name: Mach Number (Ma)
desc: Dimensionless ratio of local flow speed (V) to the speed of sound (c) in that fluid.
validations: 📌 Sonic flow (Ma = 1), Subsonic (Ma < 1), Supersonic (Ma > 1).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Don't assume gases are ALWAYS compressible! If Ma < 0.3 (like air under 100 m/s), density changes are < 5% and the gas MUST be treated as an INCOMPRESSIBLE fluid.

name: Specific Weight (γ)
desc: The weight of a unit volume of a substance.
validations: 📌 Standard gravity 'g' is approx 9.81 m/s² at sea level.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Density (ρ) is constant, but Specific Weight (γ) changes with location because 'g' changes! Never confuse it with Specific Gravity.

name: No-Slip Condition
desc: A fluid in direct contact with a solid surface 'sticks' to it due to viscous effects, causing the boundary layer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The absolute velocity of the fluid isn't always zero! The RELATIVE velocity is zero. If a plate is moving at 5 m/s, the fluid particle exactly at the wall is ALSO moving at 5 m/s.

name: Dimensional Homogeneity
desc: All additive terms in an engineering equation must have the exact same primary dimensions.
validations: 📌 Used strictly to catch errors in formula derivation.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: You CANNOT add standard Energy (kJ) with Specific Energy (kJ/kg) directly! Always multiply the specific term by mass (m) first before adding. (Classic silly mistake in Thermodynamics/Fluid equations).

name: Newton's Unity Conversion (Force)
desc: The fundamental conversion ratios for force in SI and English systems.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In the English system, never cancel lbf with lbm directly! You MUST use the conversion factor gc = 32.174 (lbm·ft)/(lbf·s²) to balance the units.

name: Ideal Gas Equation of State
desc: Relates absolute pressure, density, and absolute temperature for a gas.
validations: 📌 R is the specific gas constant (Universal Gas Constant / Molar Mass).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Temperature MUST be in Kelvin (or Rankine) and Pressure MUST be Absolute! Never use Gauge pressure or Celsius here.

name: Coefficient of Compressibility (Bulk Modulus, K)
desc: Measures a fluid's resistance to compression. Higher K means more incompressible.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For an IDEAL GAS, the isothermal bulk modulus K is exactly equal to its absolute pressure (K = P). Don't waste time deriving it in the exam!

name: Coefficient of Volume Expansion (β)
desc: Measures how much density changes with temperature at constant pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For an IDEAL GAS, β = 1/T. Students often put T in Celsius here. It MUST be in Kelvin! If T = 27°C, β = 1/300 K⁻¹, NOT 1/27.

name: Newton's Law of Viscosity
desc: Relates shear stress to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) for Newtonian fluids.
validations: 📌 μ is Dynamic Viscosity (Pa·s). 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Temperature effect is a classic MSQ! Liquid viscosity DECREASES with temp (cohesive forces weaken). Gas viscosity INCREASES with temp (molecular collisions increase).

name: Kinematic Viscosity (ν)
desc: Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density. Represents momentum diffusivity.
validations: 📌 Unit is m²/s. 1 Stoke (St) = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For gases, dynamic viscosity (μ) is mostly independent of pressure, but kinematic viscosity (ν) DEPENDS strongly on pressure because density (ρ) changes!

name: Pressure Jump Across Curved Surface
desc: The pressure difference between the inside and outside of a curved liquid interface.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A soap 'bubble' has TWO surfaces (inner and outer), hence 4σ/R. A liquid 'droplet' only has ONE surface, hence 2σ/R. Also, the formula uses Radius (R), examiners often give Diameter (D) to trick you.

name: Capillary Rise/Fall (h)
desc: The elevation or depression of a liquid in a narrow tube due to surface tension.
validations: 📌 ϕ is the contact angle.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If the contact angle ϕ > 90° (like Mercury in glass), cos(ϕ) is negative, resulting in a Capillary FALL (depression). Always double-check if the question gave 'Diameter' instead of 'Radius' (R)!

name: Cavitation Condition
desc: Formation of destructive vapor bubbles when local static pressure in a flowing liquid drops below its vapor pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Cavitation is driven by PRESSURE drops (e.g., in pump impellers or venturi throats), not just temperature increases. It causes severe structural pitting when the bubbles collapse.

name: Manometry & Pressure Drop
desc: Calculates pressure difference using a fluid column. Pressure increases going DOWN, decreases going UP.
validations: 📌 For gas flowing in a pipe, ρ_fluid is negligible compared to ρ_manometer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Always jump across the U-tube at the SAME horizontal fluid level to equate pressure. Never equate pressures across two DIFFERENT fluids at the same level!

name: Hydrostatic Force on Plane Surface
formula: F_R = P_c * A = (P_0 + ρ * g * h_c) * A
desc: Total force exerted by a static fluid on a submerged flat plate.
validations: 📌 h_c is the vertical depth to the CENTROID of the plate.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The resultant force depends ONLY on the pressure at the CENTROID (h_c), NOT the center of pressure! Do not confuse the two.

name: Center of Pressure (y_p)
desc: The exact point where the resultant hydrostatic force acts on a submerged surface.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The center of pressure (y_p) is ALWAYS below the centroid (y_c). If atmospheric pressure acts on both sides of the plate, it cancels out—so use Gage Pressure (P_0 = 0) in the formula!

name: Hydrostatic Force on Curved Surface
formula: F_H = Force on vertical projection  |  F_V = Force on horizontal projection ± Weight of fluid
desc: Breaks the force on a curved surface into horizontal and vertical components.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For F_V, the 'Weight' is the weight of the fluid block DIRECTLY ABOVE the curved surface. If the liquid is completely BELOW the curved surface (pushing up), the vertical force acts UPWARDS.

name: Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Principle)
desc: Upward force exerted by a fluid equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.
validations: 📌 For a floating body, F_B exactly equals the total weight of the body.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: ALWAYS use the density of the FLUID (ρ_fluid), NOT the solid body! And V_submerged is only the portion under the fluid level, not the total volume.

name: Fluid in Rigid-Body Linear Acceleration
desc: Calculates the angle (θ) of the free liquid surface when a container accelerates.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Pay strict attention to a_z (vertical acceleration). If the tank accelerates UPWARD, use (g + a_z). If it accelerates DOWNWARD, use (g - a_z). In free fall (a_z = -g), hydrostatic pressure variation becomes ZERO!

name: Forced Vortex (Rotating Cylinder)
desc: Calculates the maximum height difference between the edge and the center of a rotating liquid.
validations: 📌 The free surface forms a paraboloid of revolution.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The angular velocity (ω) MUST be in rad/s, not RPM! Multiply RPM by (2π/60) before putting it in the formula. Also, the volume of the empty paraboloid space is exactly HALF the volume of a cylinder with the same dimensions.

name: Material (Total) Acceleration
desc: Total acceleration of a fluid particle = Local Acceleration (time) + Convective Acceleration (space).
validations: 📌 For steady flow, the local acceleration (∂u/∂t) is zero.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A fluid particle CAN accelerate even in a purely STEADY flow! (e.g., flow through a converging nozzle). Students often mistakenly assume a = 0 just because the flow is steady.

name: Streamline Equation (2D/3D)
formula: dx / u = dy / v = dz / w
desc: Mathematical equation for a curve that is everywhere tangent to the instantaneous local velocity vector.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Streamlines, Pathlines, and Streaklines are ONLY identical in STEADY flow. If the velocity field has a 't' in it (Unsteady Flow), they are all completely different curves!

name: Continuity Equation (Incompressible Flow)
desc: The volumetric strain rate must be exactly zero for an incompressible flow field.
validations: 📌 In 2D, this reduces to (∂u/∂x) + (∂v/∂y) = 0.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Incompressibility does NOT mean velocity is constant. It just means whatever goes into a volume must come out. Always check (∂u/∂x) + (∂v/∂y) = 0 to verify if a given 2D velocity profile is physically possible!

name: Vorticity Vector (ζ)
desc: Vorticity is the curl of the velocity vector and is exactly twice the angular velocity of the fluid particle.
validations: 📌 If ζ = 0 everywhere, the flow is strictly IRROTATIONAL.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Don't confuse Vorticity with Shear Strain! Vorticity in 2D is (∂v/∂x - ∂u/∂y). It has a MINUS sign. A positive value means the fluid particle is spinning counter-clockwise.

name: Angular Velocity (2D xy-plane)
desc: Average rotation rate of two initially perpendicular lines intersecting at a point.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Students often forget the 0.5 factor! Vorticity is without the 0.5, but Angular Velocity REQUIRES the 0.5 multiplier.

name: Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) - Steady Flow
desc: Relates the rate of change of a system property to the net flux across a Control Volume boundary.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In the integral form (ρb V·n dA), the dot product V·n dictates the sign. Outflow is POSITIVE (vectors in same direction), Inflow is NEGATIVE (vectors oppose). Forgetting this sign convention ruins the entire mass/energy balance!

name: Continuity Equation (Incompressible)
desc: Conservation of mass for steady, incompressible flow in a single stream.
validations: 📌 Volume flow rate (V_dot) is conserved ONLY for incompressible fluids.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Never use A1V1 = A2V2 for GASES at high speeds! For compressible flow, you MUST include density: ρ1*A1*V1 = ρ2*A2*V2. Many students lose 2 marks ignoring density changes.

name: Bernoulli Equation
desc: Conservation of mechanical energy for steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline.
validations: 📌 Each term represents 'Head' (Energy per unit weight) in meters.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT apply Bernoulli across a pump, a turbine, or through a boundary layer! It assumes ZERO friction and ZERO shaft work. If a machine is present, you must use the General Energy Equation.

name: Stagnation Pressure (Pitot Tube)
desc: Calculates fluid velocity using the difference between stagnation and static pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Pay strict attention to the density (ρ) in the denominator. It must be the density of the FLOWING FLUID (e.g., Air), NOT the density of the manometer fluid (e.g., Water or Mercury) used to measure the pressure difference!

name: EGL and HGL (Grade Lines)
desc: EGL represents total head; HGL represents pressure + elevation head.
validations: 📌 EGL is always physically higher than HGL by the velocity head.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: EGL always drops in the direction of flow due to friction. However, HGL can actually RISE if the pipe diameter increases (since velocity drops, kinetic energy converts back to pressure).

name: General Energy Equation (Head Form)
formula: (P1/ρg) + α1(V1²/2g) + z1 + h_pump = (P2/ρg) + α2(V2²/2g) + z2 + h_turbine + h_L
desc: The complete mechanical energy balance accounting for machines and friction losses.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do not forget the Kinetic Energy Correction Factor (α)! For fully developed turbulent flow, α ≈ 1.05 (often ignored), but for fully developed LAMINAR flow, α = 2.0. Missing this completely ruins the calculation.

name: Pump & Turbine Efficiency
desc: Calculates how effectively mechanical energy is transferred between the machine and the fluid.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The formulas are inverses of each other! For a pump, fluid energy is the output (numerator). For a turbine, shaft work is the output (numerator). Never mix these up.

name: Steady Linear Momentum Equation
formula: ΣF = Σ(β * m_dot * V)_out - Σ(β * m_dot * V)_in
desc: Calculates the net force acting on a control volume due to momentum change.
validations: 📌 Force and Velocity are VECTORS. You must solve this separately for X, Y, and Z directions.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If a water jet makes a U-turn (180° bend), the outlet velocity is NEGATIVE. The formula becomes V_out - V_in = (-V) - (V) = -2V. The force doubles! Also, ALWAYS use Gage Pressures to calculate ΣF, so atmospheric pressure cancels out.

name: Momentum-Flux Correction Factor (β)
desc: Accounts for the non-uniform velocity profile across a pipe cross-section.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For turbulent flow, β ≈ 1.03 (often ignored). But for fully developed LAMINAR pipe flow, β = 4/3 (1.33). Missing this 33% increase in momentum flux is a guaranteed way to lose marks!

name: Force of Jet on Stationary Plate
formula: F_x = m_dot * V_in = ρ * A * V_in²
desc: The holding force required to keep a plate stationary when a fluid jet strikes it normally.
validations: 📌 Assumes the fluid splatters at 90° (V_out_x = 0).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the V² in the formula! If the velocity of the water jet is doubled, the required holding force is QUADRUPLED (2² = 4), not doubled. Classic GATE trick question.

name: Relative Velocity (Moving Control Volume)
desc: Fluid velocity relative to a moving control surface (like a moving plate or boat).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If a water jet strikes a MOVING plate, the mass flow rate (m_dot) striking the plate is NOT ρ*A*V_absolute. It strictly depends on the relative velocity: m_dot_strike = ρ * A * V_r. Many students forget to change m_dot!

name: Euler's Turbomachinery Formula (Angular Momentum)
desc: Calculates the shaft torque produced by a radial-flow turbine or required by a pump.
validations: 📌 V_t is the TANGENTIAL component of the absolute fluid velocity.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Radial (V_r) or Axial (V_z) velocity components create ZERO torque because their moment arm passes through the axis! Only use the Tangential component (V_t) for this equation.

name: Buckingham Pi Theorem
desc: Calculates the number of independent dimensionless groups (k) from 'n' parameters and 'j' primary dimensions.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The value of 'j' is NOT always the total number of primary dimensions (M, L, T). It is the number of primary dimensions actually PRESENT in the problem's variables. Always check if a dimension (like M) is missing before setting j=3!

name: Reynolds Number (Re)
formula: Re = (ρ * V * L) / μ = (V * L) / ν
desc: Ratio of Inertial forces to Viscous forces. Determines if flow is laminar or turbulent.
validations: 📌 For pipes, L is Diameter (D). For flat plates, L is distance from leading edge (x).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Pay close attention to whether the question gives Dynamic Viscosity (μ) in Pa·s or Kinematic Viscosity (ν) in m²/s. Mixing them up is the #1 reason for wrong Re calculations.

name: Froude Number (Fr)
desc: Ratio of Inertial forces to Gravitational forces. Crucial for open-channel and free-surface flows.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Unlike Reynolds number, Froude number has a SQUARE ROOT in the denominator. A classic mistake is calculating V / (g*L) instead of V / √(g*L).

name: Mach Number (Ma)
desc: Ratio of flow speed (V) to the local speed of sound (c). Indicates compressibility effects.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The speed of sound 'c' is NOT a constant! It changes with the fluid's temperature. Always calculate the LOCAL speed of sound first before finding the Mach number.

name: Weber Number (We)
desc: Ratio of Inertial forces to Surface Tension forces. Important for droplets, bubbles, and thin films.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the V² in the numerator! Students often forget to square the velocity when calculating the Weber number.

name: Complete Similarity
desc: For exact modeling, all independent dimensionless groups must be equal between model and prototype.
validations: 📌 Requires Geometric, Kinematic, AND Dynamic similarity.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: You cannot always match BOTH Reynolds and Froude numbers simultaneously if you use the same fluid (like water) for both model and prototype. This leads to 'Incomplete Similarity', requiring mathematical extrapolation.

name: Reynolds Number (Pipe Flow)
formula: Re = (ρ * V_avg * D) / μ = (4 * m_dot) / (π * D * μ)
desc: Ratio of inertial to viscous forces. Re < 2300 is laminar, Re > 4000 is turbulent.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The formula using mass flow rate (4*m_dot / π*D*μ) is a massive time-saver in GATE! You don't need to calculate velocity or density separately if mass flow rate is given.

name: Hydraulic Diameter (D_h)
desc: Equivalent diameter for non-circular ducts, where A_c is cross-sectional area and p is wetted perimeter.
validations: 📌 For a completely filled circular pipe, D_h = D.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: 'p' is the WETTED perimeter! If a square duct is only half-filled with liquid, you only count the three sides touching the liquid, NOT the top boundary touching the air.

name: Laminar Velocity Profile (V_avg vs V_max)
desc: In fully developed laminar pipe flow, the average velocity is exactly half the centerline (maximum) velocity.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: This ONLY applies to Laminar flow! For turbulent flow, the profile is much flatter, and V_avg is typically around 0.8 * V_max. Don't blindly divide by 2 if Re > 4000.

name: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Pressure Drop)
desc: Calculates the pressure drop due to friction in fully developed laminar pipe flow.
validations: 📌 ONLY valid for Laminar, fully developed, Newtonian fluids.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the D² in the denominator! If you reduce the pipe diameter by half, the pressure drop increases by a factor of 16. This is a classic GATE theoretical question.

name: Darcy-Weisbach Head Loss & Friction Factor
desc: Major head loss due to friction in a pipe.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: THE ULTIMATE CHEMICAL ENGINEERING TRAP! The formula 'f = 64/Re' is for the DARCY friction factor. Many ChemE textbooks use the FANNING friction factor (f = 16/Re). ALWAYS check which 'f' the examiner gave you. f_Darcy = 4 * f_Fanning!

name: Pipes in Series vs Parallel
formula: Series: Q_total = Q_1 = Q_2, h_L_total = h_L1 + h_L2 | Parallel: Q_total = Q_1 + Q_2, h_L_total = h_L1 = h_L2
desc: Rules for combining multiple pipes in a network.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In parallel pipes, the head loss (ΔP) is identical across all branches, NO MATTER their diameter or length! Fluid will automatically distribute its flow rate (Q) to make the head losses equal.

name: Continuity Equation (Incompressible)
desc: The volumetric strain rate is zero. Fluid elements do not change volume as they move.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Incompressible flow ka matlab divergence zero hona hai, chahe flow STEADY ho ya UNSTEADY. Bacche aksar unsteady flow mein ∂ρ/∂t dhoondne lagte hain, par incompressible fluid ke liye density constant rahegi!

name: Stream Function (ψ) - Cartesian 2D
desc: A scalar function whose constant values define streamlines in 2D incompressible flow.
validations: 📌 Automatically satisfies the 2D incompressible continuity equation.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Sign convention ka dhyan rakhna! 'v' hamesha negative gradient hota hai (-∂ψ/∂x). Agar tune minus sign miss kiya, toh flow ki direction ulti ho jayegi. Yaad rakhna: ψ sirf 2D flow ke liye valid hai (3D ke liye nahi).

name: Stream Function (ψ) - Cylindrical Planar
desc: Stream function relations for 2D planar flow in r-θ coordinates.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Yahan (1/r) wala factor sabse bada killer hai! Radial velocity (u_r) nikalte waqt 1/r se multiply karna mat bhoolna, warna answer aur units dono galat ho jayenge.

name: Navier-Stokes Equation (Vector Form)
desc: Newton's second law applied to an incompressible, Newtonian fluid element.
validations: 📌 DV/Dt is the Material Derivative (Local + Convective acceleration).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Yeh equation sirf NEWTONIAN fluids aur constant viscosity ke liye hai. Isme non-linearity convective acceleration terms (jaise u*∂u/∂x) ki wajah se aati hai, viscous terms ki wajah se nahi!

name: Couette Flow (Parallel Plates)
desc: Steady, incompressible, laminar flow between two infinite parallel plates (one moving).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Agar pressure gradient zero hai (dP/dx = 0), toh velocity profile ekdum LINEAR hoti hai. Par agar dP/dx non-zero hai, toh profile PARABOLIC ho jayegi. Question mein carefully padhna ki 'Simple Couette' (no pressure gradient) pucha hai ya nahi.

name: Poiseuille Flow (Pipe Flow Profile)
desc: Exact solution for steady, incompressible, laminar flow in a long round pipe.
validations: 📌 Maximum velocity occurs at r = 0 (centerline).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Boundary conditions yaad rakhna! Wall par (r = R) velocity zero hoti hai (No-slip), aur center par (r = 0) shear stress zero hota hai. Centerline velocity exactly double hoti hai average velocity ki (V_max = 2*V_avg).

name: Creeping Flow (Stokes Flow)
desc: Drag force on a sphere when viscous forces completely dominate inertial forces (Re << 1).
validations: 📌 Inertial terms are completely neglected in the N-S equations.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice that DENSITY (ρ) is completely missing from this formula! In creeping flow, aerodynamic drag is 100% independent of fluid density. Don't let the examiner trick you by giving density values in the question.

name: Irrotational Flow (Laplace Equation)
desc: In 2D irrotational and incompressible flow, both velocity potential (ϕ) and stream function (ψ) satisfy Laplace's equation.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: D'Alembert's Paradox! Potential flow theory always predicts exactly ZERO aerodynamic drag on any non-lifting body of any shape. If a question asks for drag using ONLY potential flow assumptions, the answer is always zero.

name: Boundary Layer Thickness (Laminar Flat Plate)
desc: The Blasius solution for the physical thickness (δ) of a laminar boundary layer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Boundary layer thickness (δ) grows proportional to √x, NOT x! If you double the distance from the leading edge, the boundary layer does NOT double in thickness; it increases by a factor of 1.414 (√2).

name: Displacement Thickness (δ*)
desc: The distance the solid boundary would need to be displaced to account for the mass flow reduction due to the boundary layer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Never confuse this with Momentum Thickness (θ)! Displacement thickness (δ*) accounts for MASS deficit, while Momentum thickness (θ) accounts for MOMENTUM deficit. δ* is always greater than θ.

name: Momentum Thickness (θ)
desc: A measure of the loss of momentum flux due to the boundary layer.
validations: 📌 Directly related to the skin friction drag on the surface.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In the integral, the term is (u/U) * [1 - (u/U)]. Many students forget the leading (u/U) multiplier and accidentally calculate the formula for displacement thickness instead!

name: Turbulent Boundary Layer Thickness (Flat Plate)
desc: Empirical thickness approximation for a turbulent boundary layer using the 1/7th power law.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A turbulent boundary layer grows much FASTER than a laminar one. Laminar grows as x^(1/2) (or x^0.5), while turbulent grows as x^(6/7) (or x^0.857). This is a very common theoretical comparison question.

name: Drag and Lift Coefficients (CD, CL)
desc: Dimensionless coefficients representing the drag and lift forces acting on a body.
validations: 📌 The term (0.5 * ρ * V²) is the dynamic pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The Reference Area 'A' changes based on the object! For bluff bodies (cylinders, cars, spheres), 'A' is the Frontal Area (projected area). But for airfoils and wings, 'A' is the Planform Area (top-down view). Mixing this up guarantees a -0.66 in GATE.

name: Average Friction Coefficient (Laminar Flat Plate)
desc: Calculates the AVERAGE friction drag over the entire length L of a flat plate in laminar flow.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do not confuse AVERAGE coefficient (C_f = 1.33 / √Re_L) with LOCAL coefficient (C_f,x = 0.664 / √Re_x). The average friction coefficient over the whole plate is exactly TWICE the local friction coefficient at the end of the plate!

name: Stokes' Law Drag Coefficient
desc: The drag coefficient for a smooth sphere in strictly creeping flow.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: This formula is ONLY valid for highly viscous or very slow flows where Re ≤ 1. If Re > 1, flow separation begins at the rear of the sphere, pressure drag kicks in, and you can no longer use 24/Re.

name: Minimum Flight Velocity (Takeoff Speed)
desc: The absolute minimum speed an aircraft must reach to generate enough lift to balance its weight (W).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the air density (ρ) is in the denominator! At high altitudes or on very hot summer days, air density drops significantly. This means an aircraft needs a much HIGHER takeoff speed (and a longer runway) compared to winter days at sea level.

name: Drag Crisis (Bluff Bodies)
desc: The flow region where the drag coefficient of a bluff body suddenly and massively drops.
validations: 📌 Occurs because the boundary layer transitions from laminar to turbulent.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Counter-intuitive concept! Making a surface ROUGH (like dimples on a golf ball) actually REDUCES total drag for bluff bodies. The roughness trips the boundary layer into turbulence earlier, which delays flow separation and drastically shrinks the low-pressure wake behind the ball.

name: Stagnation Temperature (T0)
desc: The temperature an ideal gas reaches when brought to rest adiabatically.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Unit mismatch is the biggest killer here! The term V²/(2) will give you J/kg. You MUST ensure your specific heat (c_p) is also in J/(kg·K), NOT kJ/(kg·K). Otherwise, your dynamic temperature term will be off by a factor of 1000!

name: Speed of Sound (Ideal Gas)
desc: The speed of an infinitesimally small pressure wave through a medium.
validations: 📌 'T' must be absolute temperature (Kelvin or Rankine).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The gas constant 'R' must be the SPECIFIC gas constant (R_universal / Molecular Weight) in J/(kg·K), not the universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K). Using the wrong 'R' will ruin your Mach number calculation.

name: Choked Flow (Converging Nozzle)
desc: The critical pressure required at the throat to achieve Mach 1 (Choked Flow).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If the back pressure (Pb) drops BELOW the critical pressure (P*), the flow rate WILL NOT increase! The nozzle is choked. The pressure at the exit plane remains stuck at P*, regardless of how low Pb gets.

name: Normal Shock Waves
desc: An abrupt, irreversible change in fluid properties where flow goes from supersonic to subsonic.
validations: 📌 Stagnation Temperature (T0) remains CONSTANT across a normal shock.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Stagnation Pressure (P0) DOES NOT remain constant across a shock wave! Because the shock is highly irreversible (entropy increases), the stagnation pressure always DROPS (P02 < P01).

name: Rayleigh Flow vs Fanno Flow
formula: Rayleigh: Heat Transfer (Q ≠ 0, Friction = 0) | Fanno: Friction (f ≠ 0, Q = 0)
desc: 1D flow models. Rayleigh involves heat addition/rejection, Fanno involves wall friction.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In BOTH flows, the effects drive the flow towards Mach 1 (Choking). Friction accelerates subsonic flow and decelerates supersonic flow. Heating accelerates subsonic flow and decelerates supersonic flow. Both lines converge at Ma = 1 on a T-s diagram.

name: Froude Number (Open Channel)
desc: Ratio of inertial forces to gravity forces. Determines the flow regime in an open channel.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For open channels, the characteristic length in the Froude number is the FLOW DEPTH (y), NOT the hydraulic radius (R_h) or hydraulic diameter! Using R_h here is a guaranteed way to get a negative mark.

name: Hydraulic Radius (R_h)
desc: The ratio of the cross-sectional area (A_c) to the wetted perimeter (p).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT confuse Hydraulic Radius (R_h) with Hydraulic Diameter (D_h)! For a fully flowing circular pipe, D_h = D, but R_h = D/4. They are off by a factor of 4. Always check which one the formula requires.

name: Minimum Specific Energy (E_s,min)
desc: The minimum specific energy required to pass a given flow rate, occurring exactly at the critical depth.
validations: 📌 Only valid for rectangular channels.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Specific Energy is measured relative to the CHANNEL BOTTOM, not a fixed horizontal datum. If the channel bottom goes up or down (like a bump), the specific energy changes, but the total energy (H) might remain constant.

name: Hydraulic Jump (Depth Ratio)
desc: Calculates the downstream depth (y2) after a hydraulic jump given the upstream depth (y1) and Froude number (Fr_1).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A hydraulic jump CANNOT occur if the upstream flow is subcritical (Fr_1 < 1). The formula mathematically breaks or gives physically impossible results if you plug in Fr_1 < 1. It ALWAYS goes from Supercritical -> Subcritical.

name: Manning Equation (Velocity)
desc: Empirical formula calculating the average velocity in uniform open-channel flow.
validations: 📌 'a' is 1 for SI units and 1.486 for English units.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the exponents! Hydraulic radius (R_h) is raised to the power of 2/3, and the bottom slope (S0) is raised to 1/2. In the rush of the exam, students often swap these exponents or forget the (1/n) factor.

name: Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)
desc: The margin of pressure above the vapor pressure at the pump inlet required to prevent cavitation.
validations: 📌 P_inlet and P_v MUST be in ABSOLUTE pressure, not gage pressure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Available NPSH drops drastically if the liquid temperature INCREASES (because vapor pressure P_v goes up). Never forget to check the fluid temperature when calculating cavitation risk! If NPSH_available < NPSH_required, your pump will cavitate and get destroyed.

name: Euler Turbomachine Equation
formula: T_shaft = ρ * Q * (r_2 * V_2,t - r_1 * V_1,t)
desc: Calculates the shaft torque required for a pump based on the change in angular momentum of the fluid.
validations: 📌 V_t is the TANGENTIAL component of the ABSOLUTE fluid velocity.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do not use the relative velocity of the fluid with respect to the blade! You must resolve the velocity triangle to find the absolute tangential velocity (V_t) to plug into this equation. Mixing up absolute and relative velocity is the #1 mistake here.

name: Pump Affinity Laws
desc: Predicts the performance of geometrically similar pumps operating at different speeds (N) or sizes (D).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Look at the exponents carefully! Brake horsepower (P) scales with the CUBE of the rotational speed (N³). If you increase a pump's speed by just 10%, the power required shoots up by 33% (1.1³ = 1.331). A classic GATE numerical trap.

name: Pump Specific Speed (N_s)
desc: A dimensionless index used to classify pump impellers based on their geometry and flow type.
validations: 📌 Must strictly be evaluated at the Best Efficiency Point (BEP).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Specific speed decides the shape of your pump! Low N_s means high head/low flow (Centrifugal/Radial pumps). High N_s means low head/high flow (Axial pumps). Examiners will give you Q, H, and N and ask you to select the correct pump type.

name: Pumps in Series & Parallel
formula: Series: H_tot = ΣH (at same Q)  |  Parallel: Q_tot = ΣQ (at same H)
desc: Rules for combining pump performance curves in a network.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Connecting TWO identical pumps in series does NOT double the flow rate in a real piping system! It only doubles the available head at a given flow. The actual new operating point depends entirely on where this new combined pump curve intersects the system's pipeline resistance curve.

name: Equiangle Skewness (Grid Quality)
desc: A metric to quantify how much a 2D mesh cell deviates from a perfect equilateral shape.
validations: 📌 For a 2D triangle, θ_equal is 60°. For a quad, it is 90°.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In CFD, a Q_EAS value of 0 means the cell is PERFECT. A value closer to 1 means it is highly distorted/skewed. Many students logically guess that '1' means 100% perfect, which is completely backwards here!

name: Strouhal Number (St)
desc: A dimensionless number describing oscillating flow mechanisms, like the Kármán vortex street behind a cylinder.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Flow around a cylinder becomes strictly UNSTEADY at Re > 90 due to alternating vortex shedding. If you force a 'Steady State' solver in CFD (or assume symmetry) for this problem, you will get a completely wrong, unphysical Drag Coefficient!

name: CFD Convergence vs Physical Accuracy
desc: The state where the iterative numerical solution stops changing significantly and satisfies the discretized equations.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Reaching mathematical 'Convergence' DOES NOT mean the answer is physically correct! If you choose the wrong turbulence model or incorrect boundary conditions, your CFD solver will perfectly converge to a 100% WRONG answer.

name: Grid Independence
desc: The condition where further refining the mesh (making cells smaller) no longer changes the final solution.
validations: 📌 You MUST prove grid independence before trusting any CFD result.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Never trust a CFD output from a single, unrefined mesh. A highly skewed unstructured grid might converge faster, but a high-quality unstructured grid is always better than a poor-quality structured grid.

name: Fourier's Law of Conduction
desc: The fundamental rate equation for heat conduction through a material.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The negative sign is crucial! Heat ALWAYS flows in the direction of DECREASING temperature. If T2 is lower than T1, (dT/dx) is negative, making the overall heat transfer rate (Q) positive in the positive x-direction.

name: Thermal Diffusivity (α)
desc: The ratio of heat conducted through a material to the heat stored per unit volume.
validations: 📌 A high α means heat propagates very quickly into the medium.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Don't confuse Thermal Conductivity (k) with Thermal Diffusivity (α). 'k' tells you how fast heat moves, but 'α' tells you how fast the material's TEMPERATURE changes. Two materials can have the same 'k' but completely different 'α' if their densities or specific heats differ.

name: Newton's Law of Cooling (Convection)
desc: The rate equation for convective heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid.
validations: 📌 'h' is NOT a property of the fluid; it depends on flow geometry and velocity.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The area 'A_s' is the SURFACE area in contact with the fluid, NOT the cross-sectional area! For a pipe, A_s is the curved surface area (π*D*L), not the cross-sectional area (π*D²/4).

name: Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiation)
desc: The net rate of radiation heat transfer between a gray surface and its surroundings.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: FATAL ERROR ALERT! Temperatures in radiation formulas MUST be in absolute scale (Kelvin or Rankine). You CANNOT use Celsius. (100°C)⁴ - (20°C)⁴ is NOT equal to (373 K)⁴ - (293 K)⁴. Always convert to Kelvin first!

name: Steady Flow Energy Balance
formula: Q_dot = m_dot * Δh = m_dot * C_p * (T_out - T_in)
desc: The first law of thermodynamics applied to a steady-flow system with only sensible heat transfer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For liquids and solids, C_p ≈ C_v ≈ C, so you just use 'C'. But for IDEAL GASES in a steady flow system (like air in a duct heating up), you MUST use C_p (constant pressure specific heat), NOT C_v, because the work of flow (Pv) is included in the enthalpy term.

name: General 1D Heat Conduction Equation
desc: The universal 1D heat conduction equation. Set n=0 for plane wall (r=x), n=1 for cylinder, n=2 for sphere.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If the problem says 'Steady State', the entire Right Hand Side (RHS) becomes ZERO. If it says 'No Heat Generation', g_dot becomes ZERO. Do not blindly integrate the full equation; cancel out the zero terms first to save 5 minutes of exam time!

name: Max Temperature Rise (Uniform Heat Generation)
desc: Calculates the temperature difference between the center and the surface of a solid with uniform heat generation.
validations: 📌 Shape_Factor: Plane Wall = 2 (use L instead of r_o), Cylinder = 4, Sphere = 6.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For a plane wall of total thickness 2L, the formula uses 'L' (the half-thickness or distance from midplane to surface). If the question gives a wall of thickness 10 cm, you MUST plug in L = 5 cm (0.05 m) into the formula ΔT = (g_dot * L²)/(2k), NOT 10 cm!

name: Thermal Symmetry Boundary Condition
desc: Mathematically identical to a perfectly insulated boundary. Heat cannot cross a plane of thermal symmetry.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In heat generation problems (like a heated plate cooled on both sides), the maximum temperature ALWAYS occurs at the plane of symmetry. This means at T_max, the temperature gradient (dT/dx) is exactly zero. This is your primary boundary condition to find integration constants!

name: Fourier-Biot Equation (3D Constant Properties)
desc: The 3D heat conduction equation for a material with constant thermal conductivity (k).
validations: 📌 ∇²T is the Laplacian of Temperature (∂²T/∂x² + ∂²T/∂y² + ∂²T/∂z²).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do not confuse this with the Laplace Equation! The Laplace equation (∇²T = 0) is ONLY for Steady-State, 3D/2D conduction with NO heat generation. The Fourier-Biot equation is the parent equation that includes both transient effects and heat generation.

name: Average Thermal Conductivity (k_ave)
desc: Used when thermal conductivity varies linearly with temperature: k(T) = k_0 * (1 + βT).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: You can only evaluate k at the average temperature (T1+T2)/2 if the variation is strictly LINEAR. If k(T) is a quadratic or exponential function of T, you CANNOT simply average the temperatures; you must formally integrate ∫k(T)dT from T1 to T2.

name: Critical Radius of Insulation (r_cr)
desc: The outer radius where adding insulation maximizes heat transfer instead of reducing it.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The ultimate paradox! Adding insulation to a very thin wire (where r_bare < r_cr) actually INCREASES heat loss! This happens because the outer surface area grows so fast that it drops the convection resistance more than the insulation adds to the conduction resistance.

name: Conduction Resistance (Cylinder)
formula: R_cyl = ln(r_2 / r_1) / (2 * π * k * L)
desc: Thermal resistance to radial heat conduction through a cylindrical layer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: NEVER use the plane wall formula (R = L/kA) for cylinders or spheres! The area is not constant; it increases as you move outwards. You must use the logarithmic formula for cylinders.

name: Fin Effectiveness (ε_fin)
desc: Measures how much the fin actually enhances heat transfer compared to the bare base area.
validations: 📌 A fin is only justified if ε_fin is significantly greater than 1 (usually > 2).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Don't confuse Effectiveness (ε) with Efficiency (η). Efficiency is always LESS than 100% (it compares the fin to a 'perfect' isothermal fin). Effectiveness MUST be GREATER than 1, otherwise your fin is acting like an insulator!

name: Radiation Heat Transfer Coefficient (h_rad)
desc: An artificial coefficient created to linearize radiation so it can be added to convection in a resistance network.
validations: 📌 R_rad = 1 / (h_rad * A_s)
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: When calculating h_rad, T_s and T_surr MUST be in absolute temperatures (Kelvin). If you plug Celsius into this bracket multiplier, your combined resistance network will completely collapse.

name: Thermal Contact Resistance (R_c)
desc: The resistance to heat flow caused by microscopic air gaps when two solid surfaces are pressed together.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Even if two metal plates look perfectly smooth, microscopically they only touch at a few peaks. The valleys trap air (which is a terrible conductor, k ≈ 0.026 W/mK). This is exactly why we use thermal paste on computer CPUs—to replace the air gaps with a highly conductive fluid!

name: Biot Number (Lumped System)
desc: Determines if internal temperature gradients are negligible (Bi ≤ 0.1).
validations: 📌 For Lumped Analysis, L_c MUST be Volume / Surface Area.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: L_c is NOT always the radius or thickness! For a sphere, L_c = (4/3 π r³) / (4 π r²) = r/3. For a long cylinder, L_c = r/2. Using just 'r' will give you the wrong Biot number and ruin the whole problem.

name: Fourier Number (τ)
desc: Dimensionless time ratio of heat conducted to heat stored.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the numerator uses Thermal Diffusivity (α = k/ρCp), NOT Thermal Conductivity (k)! Mixing up α and k in the pressure of an exam is a classic silly mistake.

name: One-Term Approximation (Heisler)
formula: θ(x,t) ≈ A1 * e^(-λ1² * τ) * cos(λ1 * x/L)
desc: Simplified analytical solution for transient conduction with spatial effects.
validations: 📌 ONLY valid if Fourier number (τ) > 0.2.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If the question asks for the temperature after a VERY short time (τ < 0.2), you CANNOT use the one-term equation or Heisler charts. The infinite series hasn't converged yet. You must use the Semi-Infinite solid equations instead!

name: Multidimensional Transient Conduction
desc: The solution for a 2D/3D body is the product of its 1D intersection geometries.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: You MUST multiply the DIMENSIONLESS temperatures (θ), NOT the actual absolute temperatures (T)! First find θ for each 1D shape, multiply them to get θ_total, and THEN convert back to the actual temperature T(x,y,t).

name: Semi-Infinite Solid
desc: Used for very thick bodies or very short times where the heat wave hasn't reached the other side.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The error function erf(∞) = 1. This means deep inside the solid (x → ∞), the temperature ALWAYS remains at the initial temperature T_i. If the heat wave reaches the back wall, it is no longer a semi-infinite solid!

name: Fourier Mesh Number (τ)
desc: A dimensionless parameter used in transient numerical heat conduction to relate time step and spatial step.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do not confuse this with the analytical Fourier number (τ = αt/L²). The numerical mesh Fourier number uses the TIME STEP (Δt) and GRID SPACING (Δx), not the total time and total macroscopic length!

name: Stability Criterion (Explicit Method)
desc: The mathematical limit for the time step (Δt) to prevent a numerical solution from blowing up.
validations: 📌 The explicit method is conditionally stable. The implicit method is unconditionally stable.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If you blindly choose a huge time step (Δt) in the explicit method just to make the computer finish faster, τ will exceed the limit. The node temperatures will start oscillating wildly into physically impossible values (divergence)!

name: Insulated Boundary Node (Mirror Concept)
desc: To write a finite difference equation for an insulated edge (Node 0), imagine a fictitious 'mirror' node (-1) outside the wall.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: When deriving the equation for an insulated Node 0, students often just delete the outside temperature term. WRONG! You must replace T_(-1) with T_1, which changes the formula to: 2*T_1 - 2*T_0 + (Heat Gen Term) = 0.

name: The Numerical Error Paradox
formula: Total Error = Discretization Error + Round-off Error
desc: The two main sources of error when using computers to solve differential equations.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A classic theoretical trick question! Making your grid size (Δx) and time step (Δt) infinitely small does NOT give you a perfectly accurate answer. While Discretization error decreases, the computer's Round-off error skyrockets due to millions of extra calculations. There is always an 'optimum' step size.

name: 1D Finite Difference (2nd Derivative)
desc: The core algebraic approximation used to replace the differential conduction equation.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the negative sign is on the center node (-2*T_m), while the neighboring nodes are positive. Messing up these signs will completely invert the heat flow direction in your solution matrix!

name: Nusselt Number (Nu)
desc: Dimensionless convection heat transfer coefficient. Represents the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer across the fluid layer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: THE BIGGEST MISTAKE IN HEAT TRANSFER! The 'k' in the Nusselt number formula is the thermal conductivity of the FLUID, not the solid surface! In the Biot number (Bi = hL/k), 'k' is for the SOLID. Mixing these up is a guaranteed -1.33 marks in GATE.

name: Prandtl Number (Pr)
desc: Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity. Links fluid mechanics to heat transfer.
validations: 📌 For gases Pr ≈ 1, Oils Pr > 1, Liquid Metals Pr < 1.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Prandtl number is PURELY A FLUID PROPERTY! Unlike Reynolds or Nusselt numbers, it does NOT depend on the flow velocity (V) or the geometry length (L_c). It only changes if the fluid's temperature or state changes.

name: Boundary Layer Thickness Ratio (δ / δ_t)
desc: Describes how the velocity boundary layer (δ) compares to the thermal boundary layer (δ_t).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For Liquid Metals, Pr << 1, meaning heat diffuses much faster than momentum. Therefore, the thermal boundary layer is much THICKER than the velocity boundary layer (δ_t > δ). For heavy oils (Pr >> 1), it's the exact opposite!

name: Chilton-Colburn Analogy
desc: Relates fluid friction (momentum transfer) to convection heat transfer. Useful when only drag force is known.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: This analogy ONLY works when pressure drag (form drag) is practically zero, like flow over a flat plate! Do not use this analogy for flow over bluff bodies (cylinders, spheres) where form drag dominates over skin friction.

name: No-Temperature-Jump Condition
desc: Equates convection into the fluid bulk with conduction at the exact solid-fluid interface.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A classic theoretical trick question! Examiners often ask 'What is the mode of heat transfer EXACTLY AT the solid-fluid interface in forced convection?' The answer is CONDUCTION, not convection, because the fluid layer directly touching the wall is perfectly stagnant due to the no-slip condition!

name: Film Temperature (T_f)
desc: The arithmetic average temperature used to evaluate fluid properties (ρ, μ, k, Pr) in external flow.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT evaluate fluid properties at the free-stream temperature (T_∞) or the surface temperature (T_s)! The boundary layer has a temperature gradient, so you must use the Film Temperature (T_f) to look up properties in the tables.

name: Critical Reynolds Number (Flat Plate)
desc: The Reynolds number at which flow over a flat plate transitions from laminar to turbulent.
validations: 📌 This is for a flat plate. For flow INSIDE a pipe, Re_cr ≈ 2000-2300.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Never use the pipe flow transition number (2000) for a flat plate! A flat plate flow remains laminar all the way up to Re = 500,000.

name: Local vs Average Friction Coefficient (Flat Plate)
desc: Formulas for skin friction drag on a flat plate in laminar flow.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice that the AVERAGE friction coefficient (C_fL) over the entire plate length L is exactly TWICE the LOCAL friction coefficient (C_fx) at the very end of the plate (x = L). Read the question carefully: are they asking for the drag at a specific point, or the total drag on the plate?

name: Tube Banks: Maximum Velocity (V_max)
desc: The velocity used to calculate the Reynolds number for flow across tube banks.
validations: 📌 For in-line arrays, this always occurs at the transverse plane.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For tube banks, you NEVER use the approach velocity (V) to calculate the Reynolds number! You must calculate V_max, which occurs at the narrowest bottleneck between the tubes. Using just 'V' will give you a completely wrong Nusselt number.

name: Flow Separation (Drag Crisis)
desc: The phenomenon where a bluff body experiences a sudden decrease in pressure drag.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Counter-intuitive! Transitioning to a TURBULENT boundary layer on a cylinder or sphere actually DECREASES the total drag. A turbulent boundary layer has more momentum, so it resists separation longer, shrinking the low-pressure wake behind the body. This is why golf balls have dimples!

name: Hydraulic Diameter (D_h)
desc: The characteristic length used to calculate Reynolds number for flow in non-circular ducts.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For a rectangular duct (width 'a', height 'b'), D_h = 4*(a*b) / (2a + 2b) = 2ab / (a+b). Many students just take the average (a+b)/2, which is completely WRONG!

name: Hydrodynamic vs Thermal Entry Length
desc: Relates the length required for the temperature profile to develop compared to the velocity profile.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For heavy oils (Pr >> 1), the velocity profile develops much faster than the temperature profile (L_t >> L_h). For liquid metals (Pr << 1), the temperature profile develops almost instantly while the velocity is still developing (L_h >> L_t).

name: Constant Surface Heat Flux (Pipe Flow)
desc: The mean fluid temperature increases linearly along the pipe when subjected to a constant uniform heat flux.
validations: 📌 In the fully developed region, the surface temperature T_s(x) also increases linearly, parallel to T_m(x).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A classic conceptual question! If heat flux is constant, the difference between surface temperature and mean fluid temperature (T_s - T_m) becomes strictly CONSTANT in the fully developed region. They rise together perfectly parallel.

name: Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)
desc: The exact average temperature difference used for heat transfer calculations when T_s is constant.
validations: 📌 ΔT_i = T_s - T_m,i and ΔT_e = T_s - T_m,e
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Never use the simple arithmetic mean ΔT = (ΔT_i + ΔT_e)/2 if the fluid temperature changes significantly. The fluid temperature approaches the wall temperature EXPONENTIALLY, so the arithmetic mean will massively overestimate the heat transfer!

name: Dittus-Boelter Equation (Turbulent Flow)
desc: The most common empirical correlation for fully developed turbulent flow in smooth pipes.
validations: 📌 n = 0.4 for heating the fluid (T_s > T_m), n = 0.3 for cooling the fluid (T_s < T_m).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT use this equation for Laminar flow (Re < 2300)! For fully developed laminar flow with constant surface temperature, Nu is a constant 3.66, regardless of Reynolds or Prandtl numbers!

name: Grashof Number (Gr)
desc: The dimensionless ratio of buoyancy forces to viscous forces in natural convection.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For an IDEAL GAS, the volume expansion coefficient (β) is EXACTLY equal to 1/T_film (where T_film must be in Kelvin!). Do not search the property tables for β if the fluid is air acting as an ideal gas; just calculate 1/T_film.

name: Rayleigh Number (Ra)
desc: The product of Grashof and Prandtl numbers. It dictates the flow regime (laminar vs turbulent) in natural convection.
validations: 📌 For a vertical plate, transition to turbulent flow occurs around Ra_L ≈ 10^9.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT use Reynolds Number (Re) to determine if natural convection is laminar or turbulent! Reynolds number requires a bulk fluid velocity (V), which doesn't exist in pure natural convection. Always use Rayleigh Number (Ra) instead.

name: Combined Natural & Forced Convection
desc: Determines whether a flow is dominated by forced convection, natural convection, or both.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If Gr / Re² ≈ 1, BOTH natural and forced convection are significant. If they are in the same direction (assisting flow), you ADD their effects. If they oppose each other (like hot fluid being forced downwards), you SUBTRACT their effects!

name: Effective Thermal Conductivity (k_eff)
desc: Used for natural convection INSIDE enclosures (like double-pane windows). It treats the convective heat transfer as pure conduction with an artificially enhanced conductivity.
validations: 📌 If Ra_L is small (usually < 1708 for horizontal enclosures), k_eff = k (pure conduction).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If a problem asks for heat transfer across a vertical air gap (like a window) and gives you a Nusselt number (Nu = 1.4), just remember that k_eff = k * Nu. You can then plug k_eff directly into the standard 1D conduction formula!

name: Characteristic Length (Horizontal Plate)
desc: The characteristic length for natural convection over a horizontal flat surface.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do NOT use the total length or width of the plate! For a horizontal square plate of side 'L', L_c = L² / 4L = L/4. For a horizontal circular disk of diameter 'D', L_c = (πD²/4) / (πD) = D/4. Using the full length/diameter will ruin your Grashof calculation.

name: The Boiling Curve (Regimes)
formula: Natural Convection ➔ Nucleate ➔ Transition ➔ Film Boiling
desc: The relationship between heat flux and excess temperature (ΔT_excess = T_s - T_sat).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The highest heat transfer coefficient (h) occurs in the NUCLEATE boiling regime, not film boiling! In film boiling, a continuous vapor film covers the heater, acting as a massive thermal insulator, causing the heat flux to actually DROP (Transition regime) before rising again due to radiation.

name: Critical (Maximum) Heat Flux (q_max)
formula: q_max = C_cr * h_fg * [σ * g * ρ_v² * (ρ_l - ρ_v)]^(1/4)
desc: The absolute maximum heat flux achievable in nucleate boiling before the vapor film blankets the surface (Burnout point).
validations: 📌 C_cr ≈ 0.15 for large horizontal plates.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice what is MISSING from this formula! q_max is completely INDEPENDENT of the fluid's viscosity (μ), thermal conductivity (k), and specific heat (C_p). It depends purely on fluid density, surface tension, and latent heat.

name: Leidenfrost Point (Minimum Heat Flux)
desc: The point on the boiling curve where transition boiling ends and stable film boiling begins.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: This is the exact point where water droplets 'dance' on a very hot pan instead of evaporating instantly! The vapor film physically lifts the droplet, insulating it from the hot surface.

name: Modified Latent Heat of Vaporization (h*_fg)
desc: Used in condensation calculations to account for the additional heat released when the liquid film cools below the saturation temperature.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Standard h_fg only accounts for the phase change from vapor to liquid at T_sat. But the liquid film on the wall is cooler than T_sat (it approaches T_s). If you don't use h*_fg, you will underpredict the total heat transfer rate!

name: Film vs Dropwise Condensation
desc: The two modes of condensation. Dropwise occurs on non-wettable surfaces; Film occurs on wettable surfaces.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Dropwise condensation is vastly superior for heat transfer because the bare metal surface is constantly re-exposed. However, in 99% of industrial condenser designs, we ASSUME Film Condensation because dropwise is almost impossible to sustain reliably over long periods without special (and expensive) surface coatings.

name: Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Blackbody)
desc: The total maximum radiation energy emitted by a blackbody per unit time per unit surface area.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The temperature 'T' MUST be in absolute units (Kelvin or Rankine). If you put Celsius or Fahrenheit into a T^4 equation, your answer won't just be a little wrong; it will be astronomically wrong!

name: Wien's Displacement Law
desc: Calculates the wavelength at which a blackbody emits the maximum amount of radiation at a given temperature.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: As temperature INCREASES, the peak wavelength DECREASES. This inverse relationship is why a piece of metal glows dull red when heated, but turns bright white (shorter wavelengths) as it gets even hotter.

name: Solid Angle (ω)
formula: dω = dA_n / r² = sin(θ) dθ dΦ
desc: The 3D equivalent of a 2D plane angle, used to calculate how much radiation intensity hits a specific area.
validations: 📌 A full sphere is 4π steradians (sr). A hemisphere is 2π sr.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The area 'dA_n' MUST be the projected area NORMAL (perpendicular) to the line of sight. If the receiving surface is tilted at an angle α, you must use dA * cos(α) in the numerator!

name: Emissive Power vs Radiation Intensity
desc: Relates the total hemispherical emissive power (E) to the directional radiation intensity (I_e) for a diffuse emitter.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Why is it π and not 2π (since a hemisphere has 2π steradians)? Because of Lambert's Cosine Law! The intensity hitting the edges of the hemisphere is reduced by cos(θ). Integrating I_e * cos(θ) over a hemisphere mathematically results in exactly π * I_e.

name: Kirchhoff's Law of Radiation
formula: ε(λ, θ, T) = α(λ, θ, T)
desc: States that the emissivity of a surface is exactly equal to its absorptivity at the same wavelength, direction, and temperature.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The general form (ε = α) is ONLY strictly valid when the surface is in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings (Surface Temp = Surroundings Temp). We often assume ε = α for 'Gray' surfaces to simplify math, but in reality, a surface that absorbs high-temp solar radiation (low λ) may have a very different emissivity for low-temp room radiation (high λ).

name: View Factor Summation Rule
desc: Conservation of energy: all radiation leaving surface 'i' must hit some surface in the enclosure.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For a plane or convex surface, F_ii = 0 (it cannot see itself). But for a CONCAVE surface, F_ii ≠ 0! If you forget to include F_ii in your summation for a concave bowl or cylinder, your entire view factor matrix will be wrong.

name: Reciprocity Relation
desc: The most powerful shortcut to find an unknown view factor when areas and the inverse view factor are known.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: F_ij is NOT equal to F_ji unless the areas are identical (A_i = A_j). In GATE, they often give a small sphere inside a large room; always use reciprocity to find the room-to-sphere factor from the sphere-to-room factor (which is 1).

name: Radiosity (J_i)
desc: Total radiation leaving a surface per unit area (Emitted + Reflected).
validations: 📌 For a blackbody, J = E_b because reflection is zero.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: When solving radiation networks, the 'driving force' is the difference in Radiosities (J1 - J2), NOT temperatures (T1 - T2). Only for blackbodies can you directly use σT⁴.

name: Radiation Surface Resistance
desc: The internal resistance of a non-black surface to emitting radiation.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If a surface is 'Adiabatic' or a 'Reradiating Surface', its net heat transfer Q = 0. This means its Radiosity (J) is exactly equal to its blackbody emissive power (E_b). You can treat it as a floating node in your electrical network analogy.

name: Radiation Shields (N-shields)
desc: Calculates the reduction in heat transfer when N shields with equal emissivities are added.
validations: 📌 1 shield reduces heat transfer by 50%, 2 shields by 66.7%, etc.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: This simplified (N+1) formula ONLY works if all surfaces (plates and shields) have the SAME emissivity. If the emissivities are different, you MUST draw the full resistance network and sum all 2N+1 resistances manually.

name: LMTD: Phase Change Exception
desc: For condensers and boilers, the fluid undergoing phase change has a constant temperature (T_in = T_out).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: If a problem says 'steam condenses at 100°C', the flow arrangement (parallel or counter-flow) DOES NOT MATTER! The LMTD will be exactly the same for both. Don't waste time drawing different temperature profiles.

name: Maximum Possible Heat Transfer (Q_max)
desc: The theoretical maximum heat transfer that could occur in a heat exchanger with infinite area.
validations: 📌 C_min is the smaller of (m_dot * C_p)_hot and (m_dot * C_p)_cold.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Q_max is ALWAYS based on C_min, never C_max! The fluid with the smaller heat capacity rate will be the one to reach the extreme temperature first (either T_h,in or T_c,in). If you use C_max, you violate the 2nd Law of Thermodynamics.

name: Heat Capacity Rate Ratio (c)
desc: Ratio used in Effectiveness-NTU charts and formulas.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For a boiler or condenser, C_max → ∞ (because temperature doesn't change, specific heat behaves as infinite). Therefore, c = 0! The effectiveness formula drastically simplifies to ε = 1 - exp(-NTU) for phase change systems.

name: Fouling Factor (R_f)
desc: Represents the added thermal resistance due to scale, dirt, or biological growth on the tube surfaces.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: R_f is a RESISTANCE, not a convection coefficient. You ADD it to the other resistances (1/h). Do not write it as 1/R_f in the denominator! Also, watch the units carefully (m²·°C/W).

name: L'Hôpital's Rule for LMTD
formula: If ΔT_1 = ΔT_2, then ΔT_lm = ΔT_1 = ΔT_2
desc: Resolves the 0/0 mathematical error in the LMTD formula for perfectly balanced counter-flow heat exchangers.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: In a counter-flow heat exchanger where (m_dot * C_p)_hot = (m_dot * C_p)_cold, the temperature difference between the two fluids is CONSTANT everywhere! The LMTD formula will give you ln(1) = 0 in the denominator. Don't panic; the LMTD is just the arithmetic difference.

name: Concentration Boundary Condition (Interface)
desc: Determines the concentration of a species at a phase interface (e.g., air-water boundary).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Unlike temperature, species concentration is ALMOST NEVER CONTINUOUS across a phase boundary! The concentration of oxygen in the air right next to a lake is totally different from the concentration of oxygen in the water right at the surface. You MUST use Henry's Law or Raoult's Law to link them.

name: Fick's Law of Diffusion
desc: The fundamental equation for mass diffusion, directly analogous to Fourier's Law of heat conduction.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Fick's law only describes the movement of a species relative to the bulk mixture. If the entire fluid is moving (like wind blowing), you must ADD the bulk convective mass flux (ρ_A * V_bulk) to the diffusive flux to get the total mass flow rate!

name: Schmidt (Sc) vs Lewis (Le) Number
formula: Sc = ν / D_AB  |  Le = α / D_AB = Pr / Sc
desc: Dimensionless numbers linking fluid mechanics, heat transfer, and mass transfer.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Do not mix these up! Schmidt (Sc) is the mass analog to Prandtl (Pr) and links momentum to mass. Lewis (Le) links HEAT to MASS. If Le ≈ 1 (like in air-water vapor mixtures), thermal and concentration boundary layers grow at the exact same rate.

name: Mass vs Mole Fractions (Ideal Gases)
formula: y_i = P_i / P_total  (Mole/Pressure Fraction)  |  w_i = y_i * (M_i / M_mix)
desc: Converts between partial pressure, mole fractions, and mass fractions.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: For an ideal gas, Mole Fraction (y_i) is EXACTLY equal to Pressure Fraction and Volume Fraction. However, it is NEVER equal to Mass Fraction (w_i) unless all species have the exact same molar mass! Always multiply by the molar mass ratio to convert.

name: Stefan Flow (Diffusion in Stationary Gas)
desc: Occurs when a liquid evaporates into a 'stagnant' gas column above it.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: A 'stationary' gas (like air above an evaporating water tank) does NOT mean bulk velocity is zero! As water vapor diffuses upward, it physically pushes the air upward. The air diffuses back down to maintain a net zero flow of air, but the overall bulk velocity of the mixture is UPWARD.

name: Junction Temperature (T_junction)
desc: The highest temperature inside an electronic chip where the actual heat is generated.
validations: 📌 R_total = R_junction-case + R_case-ambient
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: The 'Q' here is the net heat dissipated (Electrical Power In - Non-Thermal Power Out). If a device consumes 10W of electricity but emits 2W of RF radiation (like an antenna), the heat dissipated 'Q' is only 8W, not 10W!

name: Failure Rate vs Temperature
formula: Failure Rate halves for every 10°C drop in T_junction
desc: A critical industry rule of thumb relating electronic component reliability to its operating temperature.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: This is an exponential relationship, not linear! Dropping the temperature by 20°C doesn't reduce the failure rate by 20%; it reduces it by a factor of 4 (halved, and then halved again).

name: PCB Effective Thermal Conductivity
desc: Calculates the overall thermal conductivity for heat conduction ALONG a multi-layer Printed Circuit Board.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: This weighted average formula ONLY works for conduction PARALLEL to the layers (along the board). If heat is conducting ACROSS the thickness of the board, the resistances are in series, and this formula is completely invalid!

name: Fan Placement: Inlet vs Exit
formula: Inlet Fan = Pressurizes Box | Exit Fan = Creates Vacuum
desc: Design guidelines for placing cooling fans in electronic enclosures.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Placing a fan at the exit sounds smart because it blows the hot fan-motor air outside. But this creates a vacuum inside the box, sucking in unfiltered, dusty air through every crack and seam, which eventually blankets the electronics and destroys them!

name: Thermal Constriction Resistance
desc: The extra thermal resistance caused when heat flows from a very small source (like a chip junction) into a much larger substrate.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Notice the formula has 'd' (diameter of the heat source), not area (d²)! This means constriction resistance drops very slowly as you increase the size of the heat source. It acts as a severe bottleneck in modern miniaturized electronics.

name: Fluid vs Solid (Deformation)
formula: Fluid: Stress ∝ Rate of Strain | Solid: Stress ∝ Strain
desc: A fluid deforms CONTINUOUSLY under any shear stress, whereas a solid stops deforming at a fixed strain angle.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Many students think fluids don't resist shear stress. They DO resist it! But their resistance depends entirely on the *rate* of deformation (how fast it moves), not the amount of deformation.

name: The No-Slip Condition
desc: A fluid in direct contact with a solid 'sticks' to the surface due to viscosity, having zero relative velocity.
validations: 📌 This creates the boundary layer and causes surface drag on vehicles and pipes.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Students assume the fluid is completely stationary (V=0) at the wall. It is only stationary *relative* to the wall! If the wall is a moving belt traveling at 5 m/s, the fluid touching it is ALSO moving exactly at 5 m/s.

name: Incompressible Flow Approximation
desc: Flow where fluid density remains nearly constant. Liquids are always assumed incompressible.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Assuming ALL gas flows are compressible! A gas flowing at low speeds (Mach < 0.3, roughly under 100 m/s for air) is treated as INCOMPRESSIBLE in calculations because its density changes by less than 5%.

name: Mach Number (Ma)
desc: The dimensionless ratio of the local flow velocity to the local speed of sound in that medium.
validations: 📌 Ma < 1 (Subsonic), Ma = 1 (Sonic), Ma > 1 (Supersonic).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Treating the speed of sound (c) as a universal constant! The speed of sound actually changes with the fluid's temperature. A plane flying at 300 m/s at sea level has a different Mach number than at 30,000 feet.

name: Closed System vs Control Volume
formula: Closed: Fixed Mass | Open (CV): Fixed Region
desc: A closed system tracks a fixed amount of matter, while an open system (Control Volume) tracks a fixed region in space where mass flows in and out.
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Confusing a 'Closed System' with an 'Isolated System'. In a CLOSED system, mass cannot cross the boundary, but ENERGY (heat or work) definitely can! Only an ISOLATED system blocks both.

name: Accuracy vs Precision
formula: Accuracy = Closeness to True Value | Precision = Repeatability
desc: Accuracy is how correct a measurement is (low bias error), while precision is how consistently you get the same result (random error).
trap: ⚠️ TRAP: Assuming a highly precise instrument is automatically accurate. A digital scale reading 12.0001 grams is highly precise, but if it wasn't zeroed (calibrated) properly, it will be wildly inaccurate!

name: Intensive vs Extensive Properties
formula: Intensive = Mass Independent | Extensive = Mass Dependent
desc: Intensive properties (Temp, Pressure) don't change if you cut the system in half. Extensive properties (Mass, Volume) get halved.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Thinking 'Specific Volume' or 'Specific Energy' is extensive. Anytime you divide an extensive property by mass (e.g., Volume / Mass), it becomes an INTENSIVE specific property!

name: Continuum Approximation
desc: Treating a fluid as a continuous, homogeneous matter without holes, ignoring the fact that it is made of individual moving molecules.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Assuming Continuum is always valid. It completely FAILS at very high altitudes (rarefied gas flow) or in nano-channels where the mean free path of molecules is comparable to the object's size!

name: Cavitation & Vapor Pressure
desc: If the local static pressure in a flowing liquid drops below its vapor pressure at that temperature, the liquid boils locally and forms destructive vapor bubbles.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Believing water only boils at high temperatures (like 100°C). Water can violently boil at room temperature (20°C) inside a pipe if a pump or valve causes the local pressure to drop below 2.34 kPa!

name: Coefficient of Compressibility (Bulk Modulus, K)
desc: Measures a fluid's resistance to compression. A higher K means the fluid is more incompressible (liquids have huge K values).
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Trying to derive K for an Ideal Gas from scratch. For an IDEAL GAS at constant temperature (isothermal), the bulk modulus K is EXACTLY equal to its absolute pressure (K = P). Save time in the exam!

name: Dynamic (μ) vs Kinematic (ν) Viscosity
desc: Dynamic viscosity (μ) is the internal resistance to shear. Kinematic viscosity (ν) is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density.
validations: Dynamic Units: Pa·s or Poise | Kinematic Units: m²/s or Stokes.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Mixing up the units! Questions often give viscosity in Poise or Stokes to trick you. 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s, and 1 Stoke = 10^-4 m²/s. Not converting these is the #1 reason for wrong Reynolds Number calculations.

name: Capillary Effect & Contact Angle (ϕ)
formula: Capillary Rise (h) = (2 * σ_s * cosϕ) / (ρ * g * R)
desc: The rise or fall of a liquid in a narrow tube depending on whether adhesive forces (wetting) or cohesive forces (non-wetting) dominate.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Assuming all fluids 'rise' in a capillary. If the contact angle is > 90° (like mercury on glass), cohesive forces are stronger than adhesive. The fluid does NOT wet the surface, resulting in a capillary DEPRESSION (h is negative)!

name: Absolute vs Gage Pressure
desc: Absolute pressure is measured relative to a perfect vacuum. Gage pressure is measured relative to local atmospheric pressure.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Using Gage pressure in the Ideal Gas Law (Pv = RT) or thermodynamic calculations! You MUST always use Absolute Pressure when calculating density or temperatures of gases.

name: Pascal's Law (Hydraulic Leverage)
desc: Pressure applied to a confined fluid increases the pressure everywhere by the same amount, allowing small forces to lift heavy weights.
validations: 📌 The mechanical advantage is equal to the ratio of the areas (A2/A1).
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Thinking a hydraulic lift creates 'free energy'. While the force multiplies (F2 > F1), the WORK done remains exactly the same! The smaller piston must move a much larger distance to lift the heavy weight.

name: Center of Pressure (Hydrostatic Force)
desc: The exact point where the total resultant hydrostatic force acts on a submerged plane surface.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Assuming the resultant pressure force acts at the geometric center (Centroid). Because pressure increases with depth, the Center of Pressure is ALWAYS mathematically below the centroid (unless the plate is perfectly horizontal)!

name: Archimedes' Principle (Buoyancy)
desc: The upward buoyant force equals the WEIGHT of the fluid displaced by the body.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Using the density of the *solid object* instead of the *fluid* to calculate buoyant force. Also, using the total volume instead of just the *submerged* volume! Always use ρ_fluid and V_submerged.

name: Stability of Floating Bodies (Metacenter)
formula: Stable if: Metacenter (M) is ABOVE Center of Gravity (G)
desc: A floating body is rotationally stable if a small disturbance creates a restoring moment, governed by the metacentric height (GM).
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Thinking the Center of Gravity (G) MUST be below the Center of Buoyancy (B) for stability. False! A ship floats perfectly stable even with heavy cargo on deck (G above B), as long as its METACENTER (M) remains above G.

name: Fluid Rigid-Body Motion (Isobars)
desc: When a fluid moves as a solid block (constant linear acceleration or rotation), shear stresses vanish, and pressure surfaces tilt or curve.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Assuming pressure only varies vertically. In a fluid accelerating horizontally or rotating, pressure ALSO varies radially or horizontally! A rotating coffee cup has a parabolic free surface, and pressure at the walls is much higher than at the center.

name: Lagrangian vs Eulerian Approach
formula: Lagrangian = Track Particle | Eulerian = Track Space
desc: Lagrangian tracks individual fluid particles as they move (like tracking a specific car). Eulerian anchors a probe in a fixed volume and watches fluid pass by (like a traffic camera).
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Thinking weather balloons are Eulerian! Even though they measure data, they MOVE WITH the fluid. Any probe that floats and travels with the fluid is a Lagrangian measurement. A probe fixed to the ground or a stationary airplane wing is Eulerian.

name: Material (Substantial) Derivative
desc: Transforms Lagrangian tracking into the Eulerian frame. It consists of Local change (change over time at a fixed point) and Advective change (change due to particle moving to a new location).
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Assuming acceleration is ZERO in a steady flow! In a steady flow, the *local* acceleration (∂V/∂t) is zero, but the *advective* acceleration (V·∇V) can be huge (e.g., water speeding up as it enters a narrow nozzle).

name: Streamlines, Pathlines & Streaklines
desc: Streamline: Snapshot of velocity directions. Pathline: Actual history of one particle. Streakline: Locus of all particles that passed through a single injection point (like a smoke wand).
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Forgetting the golden rule of Unsteady flow! In an unsteady (time-varying) flow, these three lines are ALWAYS completely different from each other. They only perfectly overlap in STEADY flow.

name: Volumetric Strain Rate (Dilation)
desc: The rate at which a fluid element's volume expands or shrinks as it moves.
validations: 📌 For an incompressible flow, the volumetric strain rate (divergence of velocity) is EXACTLY ZERO.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Mixing up linear strain with volumetric strain. A fluid element can be stretched in the x-direction and squashed in the y-direction (linear strains exist), but if its total volume doesn't change, the volumetric strain rate is zero!

name: Vorticity (ζ) and Rotationality
desc: Vorticity is the curl of the velocity field. It mathematically represents twice the angular velocity of a fluid particle.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Thinking 'Irrotational flow' means the fluid moves in straight lines. False! Fluid can move in a perfect circle (like a free vortex or a line sink) and still be completely IRROTATIONAL as long as the individual fluid particles aren't spinning on their own axes!

name: Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)
formula: dB_sys/dt = (d/dt ∫_CV ρb dV) + ∫_CS ρb (V_r · n) dA
desc: The ultimate bridge between System (Lagrangian) and Control Volume (Eulerian) physics. It tracks how much of an extensive property (B) changes within the CV and how much flows out.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Messing up the dot product (V_r · n) at the boundaries. The normal vector (n) always points OUTWARD. So, for flow EXITING the control volume, the term is POSITIVE. For flow ENTERING, the term is NEGATIVE.

name: Bernoulli Equation Limits & Validity
formula: Valid ONLY IF: Steady + Incompressible + Inviscid + No Shaft Work
desc: The holy grail of fluid mechanics, but it only works for idealized fluid streams where mechanical energy isn't lost to friction or changed by machines.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Applying Bernoulli across a pump, turbine, or through a boundary layer! If there's a machine or high friction (viscous region), Bernoulli fails completely. You MUST use the General Energy Equation instead.

name: Hydraulic vs Energy Grade Lines (HGL & EGL)
desc: HGL shows the static pressure + elevation. EGL shows the total mechanical energy. EGL is ALWAYS physically above HGL by a distance of V²/2g.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Thinking pipe elevation above the HGL is safe. If a physical pipe goes mathematically ABOVE its own HGL, the pressure inside drops below atmospheric (vacuum/negative pressure), which is a guaranteed recipe for catastrophic cavitation!

name: Stagnation vs Static Pressure (Pitot Tube)
desc: Static pressure is the actual fluid pressure. Stagnation pressure is the absolute max pressure felt when the fluid crashes into a dead stop (like the tip of a Pitot probe).
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Thinking a Pitot tube directly measures 'velocity'. It doesn't! A pure Pitot tube ONLY measures total stagnation pressure. You need a separate static pressure tap (or a combined Pitot-Static probe) to find the dynamic difference and calculate velocity.

name: Kinetic Energy Correction Factor (α)
desc: Corrects the kinetic energy equation (V²/2g) because real fluids don't move at a single uniform average velocity; they have parabolic or fluctuating profiles.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Ignoring 'α' in Laminar flow problems! If you just use (V_avg² / 2g) for a laminar pipe, your kinetic energy calculation will be EXACTLY 50% wrong because α = 2.0. Always multiply the velocity head by α!

name: Flow Energy (Flow Work)
desc: The invisible 'pushing' work required by the fluid behind to force a unit mass of fluid into or out of a control volume.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Adding flow energy (P/ρ) to a CLOSED system energy balance. Flow energy literally only exists at the boundary of an OPEN system (Control Volume) where fluid is actively crossing. No mass flow = No flow energy!

name: Conservation of Mass vs Volume
formula: Mass is ALWAYS conserved. Volume is NOT.
desc: What goes in must come out (in steady state) in terms of MASS (kg/s). But the volume (m³/s) can easily shrink or expand depending on density changes.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Applying Q_in = Q_out (Volume flow in = out) for air compressors or gas systems! Q_in = Q_out ONLY works for perfectly incompressible fluids (liquids). For gases, you MUST use m_in = m_out (ρ1*Q1 = ρ2*Q2) or you will get completely wrong answers.

name: Body Forces vs. Surface Forces
formula: Rule: Body = Acts on Volume | Surface = Acts on Boundaries
desc: Body forces act invisibly on the entire mass of the fluid from a distance (like gravity/weight or magnetism). Surface forces act only where the fluid physically touches a boundary (like pressure pushing on a wall or viscous friction).
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Confusing Pressure as a body force. Pressure is strictly a SURFACE force! It only pushes against the control surface boundaries, it doesn't pull the entire volume from the inside.

name: The Helicopter Hovering Paradox
formula: Rule: Lower Air Density = Higher Power Required
desc: A helicopter hovers by imparting downward momentum to the air. If the air is thin (less dense), the rotors must spin much faster and work harder to push the same mass of air.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Assuming a helicopter performs better in hot summer air. False! Hot air expands and becomes LESS dense. A helicopter engine has to work significantly harder to hover in the summer or at high mountain altitudes compared to a cold day at sea level.

name: The Rocket Exhaust Velocity Myth
formula: Rule: Rocket Velocity CAN exceed its own Exhaust Velocity
desc: A rocket propels itself by continuously throwing mass backward. Because space has no friction, the continuous thrust keeps accelerating the rocket regardless of how fast the gas is leaving the nozzle.
trap: STUDENT MISTAKE: Believing a rocket can only go as fast as the gases shooting out of its back. A rocket can easily travel much faster than its own exhaust velocity due to the continuous expulsion of mass over time!

name: The U-Turn Momentum Trap (Firehose Effect)
