formula_used: Q = ΔT / (R_conv,i + 2R_glass + R_air + R_conv,o)
trap: 🚌 Assuming the air layer resistance is negligible; forgetting that air is a good insulator when stagnant.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Steam at T∞1 = 320°C flows in a cast iron pipe (k = 80 W/m·°C) with inner radius r1 = 2.5 cm and outer radius r2 = 2.75 cm. The pipe is covered with 3-cm-thick glass wool insulation (k = 0.05 W/m·°C). Heat is lost to surroundings at T∞2 = 5°C by natural convection and radiation with combined coefficient h2 = 18 W/m²·°C. Inside convection coefficient h1 = 60 W/m²·°C. Determine the rate of heat loss per meter length and the temperature drops across the pipe and insulation.
solution_steps: Step 4: Q = (320-5)/2.61 = 121 W per meter.
Step 5: ΔT_pipe = Q R_pipe = 121×0.0002 = 0.02°C; ΔT_ins = Q R_ins = 121×2.35 = 284°C.
formula_used: R_cyl = ln(r2/r1)/(2πkL); R_conv = 1/(hA); Q = ΔT / R_total
trap: 🚨 Neglecting the insulation resistance because it's large but critical; using wrong radius for convection area.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A 3-mm-diameter and 5-m-long electric wire is tightly wrapped with a 2-mm-thick plastic cover (k = 0.15 W/m·°C). A current of 10 A passes through the wire with a voltage drop of 8 V. The insulated wire is exposed to air at 30°C with h = 12 W/m²·°C. Determine the temperature at the interface of the wire and plastic cover. Also, will doubling the thickness increase or decrease this interface temperature?
solution_steps: Step 1: Heat generated: Q = VI = 8×10 = 80 W.
Step 2: Outer radius r2 = r1 + t = 0.0015+0.002 = 0.0035 m. Outer surface area per meter: A2 = 2πr2 = 0.0220 m²/m (for L=5 m, total A2 = 0.110 m²).
Step 3: Resistances per total length: R_plastic = ln(r2/r1)/(2πkL) = ln(3.5/1.5)/(2π×0.15×5) = 0.18°C/W; R_conv = 1/(hA2) = 1/(12×0.110) = 0.76°C/W.
Step 5: Interface temperature T1 = T∞ + Q R_total = 30 + 80×0.94 = 105°C.
Step 6: Critical radius r_cr = k/h = 0.15/12 = 0.0125 m = 12.5 mm, which is larger than current r2 (3.5 mm). So increasing thickness increases heat transfer (decreases T1).
formula_used: R_cyl = ln(r2/r1)/(2πkL); R_conv = 1/(hA); r_cr = k/h
final_answer: Interface temperature = 105°C; doubling thickness will decrease interface temperature.
trap: 🚨 Forgetting that the heat generated is the total Q; using incorrect radius for convection; misapplying critical radius concept.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Steam in a heating system flows through tubes of outer diameter D1 = 3 cm, wall temperature 120°C. Circular aluminum fins (k = 180 W/m·°C) of outer diameter D2 = 6 cm and thickness t = 2 mm are attached. Fin spacing 3 mm (200 fins per meter). Surrounding air at 25°C with h = 60 W/m²·°C. Determine the increase in heat transfer per meter of tube length due to fins.
solution_steps: Step 1: Without fins: A_no_fin = π D1 L = π×0.03×1 = 0.0942 m²; Q_no_fin = h A_no_fin (Tb – T∞) = 60×0.0942×(120-25) = 537 W.

Step 2: For one fin: r1 = 0.015 m, r2 = 0.03 m, L_fin = (D2-D1)/2 = 0.015 m. Corrected length L_c = L + t/2 = 0.015 + 0.001 = 0.016 m. Parameter ξ = (L_c)√(2h/(k t)) = 0.016√(2×60/(180×0.002)) = 0.207. From fin efficiency chart, η_fin ≈ 0.95.
Step 4: Unfinned portion per fin spacing: A_unfin = π D1 × spacing = π×0.03×0.003 = 0.000283 m². Q_unfin = h A_unfin (Tb – T∞) = 60×0.000283×95 = 1.60 W.
Step 5: Total for 200 fins: Q_total = 200×(25.0+1.60) = 5320 W. Increase = 5320 – 537 = 4783 W per meter.
formula_used: η_fin = tanh(mL_c)/(mL_c); m = √(2h/(kt)); Q_fin = η_fin h A_fin (Tb - T∞)
trap: 🚨 Using actual length instead of corrected length; mis‑calculating fin area (include both sides); forgetting to account for unfinned area.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A 30-m-long, 10-cm-diameter hot water pipe is buried 50 cm below the ground surface. Outer surface temperature of pipe = 80°C, earth surface temperature = 10°C, soil thermal conductivity = 0.9 W/m·°C. Determine the rate of heat loss from the pipe.
solution_steps: Step 1: Use shape factor for an isothermal cylinder buried in a semi‑infinite medium (Table 3-5): S = 2πL / ln(4z/D) where z = depth to centerline, D = diameter. Here z = 0.5 m, D = 0.1 m, L = 30 m.
Step 3: Q = S k (T_pipe – T_surface) = 62.9 × 0.9 × (80-10) = 3963 W.
formula_used: Q = S k ΔT; S = 2πL / ln(4z/D)
trap: 🚨 Using wrong shape factor formula; forgetting that the pipe is buried, not exposed to air; incorrectly assuming one‑dimensional conduction.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Lumped System Analysis
desc: When a body's temperature remains uniform at any time, it can be analyzed as a lumped system. The temperature varies only with time, not position. This is valid when the Biot number Bi = hL_c/k < 0.1, where L_c = V/A_s is the characteristic length.
formula: T(t) - T∞ = (Ti - T∞) e^{-bt}, b = hA_s/(ρV C_p) = h/(ρ C_p L_c)
trap: 🚨 Using lumped analysis when Bi > 0.1 leads to significant error. The characteristic length L_c is V/A_s, not simply a geometric dimension.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Biot and Fourier Numbers
desc: The Biot number Bi = hL_c/k compares internal conduction resistance to external convection resistance. Small Bi (<< 0.1) allows lumped analysis. The Fourier number τ = αt/L_c² measures heat conduction relative to heat storage, indicating the time scale of transient.
formula: Bi = h L_c / k; τ = α t / L_c²
trap: 🚨 Confusing Biot number with Nusselt number; using wrong characteristic length (e.g., using half‑thickness for a plane wall when L_c = L for lumped but Bi = hL/k for wall with 2L thickness in charts).

subject: Heat Transfer
name: One‑Term Approximation
desc: For Fourier number τ ≥ 0.2, the infinite series solution for 1D transient conduction can be approximated by the first term. For a plane wall, T(x,t) = T∞ + A₁ e^{-λ₁²τ} cos(λ₁ x/L). Constants A₁, λ₁ depend on Bi and are tabulated.
formula: θ(x,t) = A₁ e^{-λ₁²τ} cos(λ₁ x/L) (wall); center: θ₀ = A₁ e^{-λ₁²τ}
trap: 🚨 Applying one‑term approximation when τ < 0.2 (error >2%). Using wrong eigenvalue for the geometry (cylinder vs. sphere).

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Heisler and Gröber Charts
desc: Graphical solutions for 1D transient conduction in plane walls, long cylinders, and spheres. They provide mid‑plane temperature (Heisler), temperature at any x (Heisler), and total heat transfer (Gröber) as functions of Bi, τ, and x/L. Valid for τ ≥ 0.2.
trap: 🚌 Reading charts inaccurately; using them when the initial temperature is not uniform or boundary conditions differ (e.g., convection vs. specified temperature).

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Semi‑Infinite Solid
desc: An idealization for a thick medium where only one surface experiences a thermal change. For convection at the surface, the temperature distribution is given by an error‑function solution. It is used for short‑time or near‑surface effects.
trap: 🚨 Using semi‑infinite model when the other boundary influences the region; forgetting that the error function arguments must be dimensionless.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Product Solution
desc: For multidimensional transient conduction (e.g., short cylinder, rectangular bar) with uniform initial temperature and same convection on all surfaces, the dimensionless temperature is the product of 1D solutions of the intersecting geometries.
formula: θ_2D(x,y,t) = θ_wall1(x,t) × θ_wall2(y,t); θ_short_cyl(r,x,t) = θ_cyl(r,t) × θ_wall(x,t)
trap: 🚨 Applying product solution when boundary conditions or initial temperatures are not the same on all faces; using incorrect 1D solutions.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Refrigeration of Foods
desc: Rapid cooling and freezing of foods slows microbial growth and extends shelf life. The cooling rate affects ice crystal size (slow freezing = large crystals, tissue damage). Properties like specific heat, thermal conductivity depend on water content and temperature.
formula: Freezing time depends on geometry, temperature, heat transfer coefficient; latent heat of fusion ~ 250 kJ/kg for meat.
trap: 🚨 Assuming properties are constant during phase change; ignoring the latent heat plateau during freezing.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: The temperature of a gas stream is to be measured by a thermocouple whose junction can be approximated as a 1-mm-diameter sphere. The properties of the junction are k = 35 W/m·°C, ρ = 8500 kg/m³, C_p = 320 J/kg·°C, and the convection heat transfer coefficient between the junction and the gas is h = 210 W/m²·°C. Determine how long it will take for the thermocouple to read 99 percent of the initial temperature difference.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute characteristic length L_c = V/A_s = D/6 = 0.001/6 = 1.667×10⁻⁴ m.
Step 2: Biot number Bi = hL_c/k = 210×1.667e-4 / 35 = 0.001 < 0.1 → lumped analysis valid.
Step 4: Condition for 99% of initial temperature difference: (T - T∞)/(Ti - T∞) = 0.01 = e^{-bt}.
formula_used: Bi = hL_c/k; b = h/(ρ C_p L_c); (T-T∞)/(Ti-T∞) = e^{-bt}
trap: 🚨 Using diameter as characteristic length instead of radius; forgetting to convert to meters; applying lumped analysis without checking Bi.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A person is found dead at 5 PM in a room whose temperature is 20°C. The temperature of the body is measured to be 25°C when found, and the heat transfer coefficient is estimated to be h = 8 W/m²·°C. Modeling the body as a 30-cm-diameter, 1.70-m-long cylinder, estimate the time of death.
solution_steps: Step 2: Bi = hL_c/k, using water properties at average temp: k ≈ 0.617 W/m·°C, ρ=996 kg/m³, C_p=4178 J/kg·°C → Bi = 8×0.0689/0.617 = 0.89 > 0.1 → lumped not strictly valid, but used for estimate.
Step 5: Time of death ≈ 5 PM – 12.2 h = 5 AM (approx).
formula_used: L_c = V/A_s; b = h/(ρ C_p L_c); (T-T∞)/(Ti-T∞) = e^{-bt}
trap: 🚌 Using body properties incorrectly (specific heat of water); applying lumped analysis despite Bi > 0.1, leading to approximate result.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: An ordinary egg can be approximated as a 5-cm-diameter sphere. The egg is initially at 5°C and is dropped into boiling water at 95°C. Convection heat transfer coefficient h = 1200 W/m²·°C. Determine how long it will take for the center of the egg to reach 70°C. (Properties: k = 0.627 W/m·°C, α = 0.151×10⁻⁶ m²/s.)
solution_steps: Step 2: From Table 4‑1 for sphere with Bi=47.8, λ₁ ≈ 3.0753, A₁ ≈ 1.9958.
formula_used: θ₀ = A₁ e^{-λ₁²τ}; τ = αt/r₀²
trap: 🚨 Using lumped analysis when Bi is large; forgetting to use absolute temperatures in exponential; mis‑reading Table 4‑1 for eigenvalues.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Large brass plates of 4 cm thickness initially at 20°C are heated in an oven at 500°C for 7 min. Combined convection/radiation coefficient h = 120 W/m²·°C. Determine the surface temperature of the plates when they come out. (Brass: k = 110 W/m·°C, α = 33.9×10⁻⁶ m²/s.)
solution_steps: Step 1: Half‑thickness L = 0.02 m. τ = αt/L² = (33.9e-6×420)/(0.02²) = 35.6. 1/Bi = k/(hL) = 110/(120×0.02) = 45.8. From Heisler chart (Fig. 4‑13a), θ₀ = (T₀ - T∞)/(Ti - T∞) ≈ 0.46.
Step 2: From Fig. 4‑13b, with x/L=1, 1/Bi=45.8, (T - T∞)/(T₀ - T∞) ≈ 0.99.
formula_used: Heisler charts: θ₀ = f(τ,Bi), θ/θ₀ = f(x/L,Bi)
trap: 🚌 Using the chart incorrectly (e.g., mixing up axes); forgetting that half‑thickness is used for plane wall; misreading the values.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A long 20-cm-diameter stainless steel 304 shaft (k=14.9 W/m·°C, α=3.95×10⁻⁶ m²/s) at 600°C is cooled in an environment at 200°C with h = 80 W/m²·°C. Determine the center temperature after 45 min and the heat transfer per unit length.
solution_steps: Step 2: From Heisler chart (Fig. 4‑14a), θ₀ = (T₀ - T∞)/(Ti - T∞) ≈ 0.40 → T₀ = 200 + 0.40×(600-200) = 360°C.
formula_used: Heisler charts for cylinder; Q/Q_max from Gröber chart; Q_max = ρV C_p (Ti-T∞)
final_answer: Center temperature = 360°C; Heat transfer = 29,360 kJ per meter length
trap: 🚨 Using plane wall charts for cylinder; misreading τ axis; forgetting to convert time to seconds.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: The ground is covered with snow at –10°C for three months. The soil initially at 15°C has k = 0.4 W/m·°C, α = 0.15×10⁻⁶ m²/s. Determine the minimum burial depth of water pipes to prevent freezing (temperature below 0°C).
solution_steps: Step 1: Treat as semi‑infinite solid with sudden surface temperature change to Ts = –10°C. We need depth x where T = 0°C after t = 90×24×3600 = 7.78×10⁶ s.
Step 2: Use solution for specified surface temperature: (T(x,t)-Ti)/(Ts-Ti) = erfc(x/(2√(αt))) = (0-15)/(-10-15) = -15/ -25 = 0.6.
formula_used: T(x,t) = Ts + (Ti - Ts) erfc(x/(2√(αt)))
trap: 🚨 Using convection boundary instead of specified temperature; forgetting to use absolute values in temperature ratio; misreading error function table.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A short brass cylinder (D = 10 cm, H = 12 cm, Ti = 120°C) is placed in air at 25°C with h = 60 W/m²·°C. Using properties: k = 110 W/m·°C, α = 33.9×10⁻⁶ m²/s, determine the temperature at the center of the cylinder and at the center of the top surface after 15 min.
solution_steps: Step 1: For plane wall (thickness 2L=12 cm, L=0.06 m): τ_wall = αt/L² = 33.9e-6×900/(0.06²) = 8.48. 1/Bi_wall = k/(hL) = 110/(60×0.06) = 30.6. From Fig. 4‑13a, θ_wall,center ≈ 0.8.
Step 2: For cylinder (r₀=0.05 m): τ_cyl = αt/r₀² = 33.9e-6×900/(0.05²) = 12.2. 1/Bi_cyl = k/(h r₀) = 110/(60×0.05) = 36.7. From Fig. 4‑14a, θ_cyl,center ≈ 0.5.
Step 3: Product solution for short cylinder center: θ_center = θ_wall,center × θ_cyl,center = 0.8×0.5 = 0.4 → T_center = 25 + 0.4×(120-25) = 63°C.
Step 4: For top surface center: x/L = 1. From Fig. 4‑13b, with 1/Bi=30.6, θ/θ₀ ≈ 0.98 → θ_wall,surface = 0.8×0.98 = 0.784.
Step 5: Cylinder center still 0.5 → product = 0.784×0.5 = 0.392 → T_top_center = 25 + 0.392×(120-25) = 62.2°C.
formula_used: θ_short_cyl = θ_wall × θ_cyl
trap: 🚨 Using wrong geometry intersection (e.g., using sphere instead of cylinder); forgetting that the top surface corresponds to x = L.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: 1‑in‑thick steaks initially at 75°F are cooled in a refrigerator at 5°F. The steak must be cooled below 45°F but not below 35°F anywhere. Determine the heat transfer coefficient h that meets both constraints while minimizing time. Properties: k = 0.26 Btu/h·ft·°F, α = 0.0035 ft²/h.
solution_steps: Step 1: For the limiting case, surface temperature T_s = 35°F, center T_c = 45°F, T∞ = 5°F, half‑thickness L = 0.5 in = 0.04167 ft.
Step 2: Using Heisler charts: At surface, (T_s - T∞)/(T_c - T∞) = (35-5)/(45-5) = 30/40 = 0.75. From Fig. 4‑13b for x/L = 1, this ratio corresponds to 1/Bi = 1.5 → Bi = 0.667.
formula_used: Bi = hL/k; Heisler chart for plane wall
trap: 🚨 Using correct half‑thickness; not mixing up center and surface temperatures; reading the chart accurately.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Why Numerical Methods Are Needed
desc: Analytical methods are limited to simple geometries and simple boundary conditions. Numerical methods allow solving complex geometries, variable properties, and non‑linear boundary conditions (e.g., radiation). They provide approximate solutions but can model real problems more accurately.
trap: 🚨 Assuming that an exact analytical solution always gives more accurate results than a numerical solution of a realistic model. A realistic numerical model often outperforms an oversimplified analytical model.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Finite Difference Formulation
desc: Derivatives are replaced by finite differences using Taylor series. The first derivative approximation: df/dx ≈ (f(x+Δx)-f(x))/Δx. The second derivative: d²T/dx² ≈ (T_{m-1} - 2T_m + T_{m+1})/Δx². The error is proportional to Δx².
trap: 🚨 Using too large a step size (Δx) increases discretization error. The approximation assumes linear variation between nodes, which may be inaccurate in regions of steep gradients.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Energy Balance Method (Control Volume)
desc: The region is divided into volume elements around nodes. An energy balance is written for each element assuming steady or transient conditions. For interior nodes with constant k and no generation, the finite difference equation reduces to T_left + T_top + T_right + T_bottom - 4T_node = 0 (2D, square mesh).
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to include heat generation in the control volume; assuming direction of heat flow incorrectly, but the final equation is unaffected if all terms are taken as into the element.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Boundary Condition Treatment (Finite Difference)
desc: Boundary nodes are handled by writing an energy balance on the half‑size volume element at the boundary. Common conditions: specified temperature (T given), specified heat flux (q̇ given), convection (hA(T∞-T_surface)), and radiation. Insulated boundaries can be treated by mirror image.
formula: Convection at left boundary: hA(T∞-T₀) + kA (T₁-T₀)/Δx + ġ₀ A Δx/2 = 0 (steady)
trap: 🚌 Using the wrong volume for boundary nodes (half‑thickness); forgetting to account for heat generation in boundary elements.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Explicit vs Implicit Methods for Transient Problems
desc: Explicit method uses temperatures from previous time step (i) to compute new temperatures (i+1); it is simple but requires small time steps for stability. Implicit method uses temperatures from the new time step (i+1), resulting in a system of equations to solve simultaneously, but it is unconditionally stable.
formula: Explicit (1D interior): T_m^{i+1} = T_m^i + τ (T_{m-1}^i - 2T_m^i + T_{m+1}^i) + (ġ_m Δx²/k); where τ = αΔt/Δx²
trap: 🚨 Using explicit method with too large a time step leads to instability and oscillatory results. The stability criterion (e.g., τ ≤ 1/2 for 1D interior) must be satisfied.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Stability Criterion for Explicit Method
desc: For the explicit method, all primary coefficients (the coefficients multiplying the node temperature at the current time step in the equation for the next time step) must be ≥ 0 to avoid divergence. For 1D interior nodes, this requires τ = αΔt/Δx² ≤ 1/2. For 2D square mesh, τ ≤ 1/4. Boundary nodes may impose stricter limits.
trap: 🚨 Using τ > 0.5 in 1D explicit can cause temperatures to oscillate and become physically impossible, violating the second law.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Discretization and Round‑Off Errors
desc: Discretization error arises from approximating derivatives by finite differences; it decreases as Δx or Δt decreases. Round‑off error arises from finite precision of computers; it increases as more calculations are performed (i.e., as mesh becomes finer). The total error has a minimum at an optimal step size.
trap: 🚨 Using an extremely fine mesh does not guarantee more accurate results because round‑off error may dominate. Always perform grid independence tests.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A large uranium plate of thickness L = 4 cm, k = 28 W/m·°C, ġ = 5×10⁶ W/m³. One side at 0°C, the other side convects to air at 30°C with h = 45 W/m²·°C. Using three equally spaced nodes (0,1,2), estimate the exposed surface temperature under steady conditions.
solution_steps: Step 1: Δx = L/(M-1) = 0.04/2 = 0.02 m. Node 0 at 0°C, node 2 exposed.
Step 2: For interior node 1: (T₀ - 2T₁ + T₂)/Δx² + ġ/k = 0 → 2T₁ - T₂ = 71.43.
Step 3: For boundary node 2 (convection): energy balance: hA(T∞-T₂) + kA(T₁-T₂)/Δx + ġ A Δx/2 = 0 → T₁ - (1 + hΔx/k)T₂ = -hΔx/k T∞ - ġΔx²/(2k). Substitute numbers: T₁ - 1.032T₂ = -36.68.
Step 4: Solve: from (1) T₁ = (71.43 + T₂)/2. Substitute: (71.43 + T₂)/2 - 1.032T₂ = -36.68 → T₂ = 136.1°C.
formula_used: Finite difference: (T_{m-1} - 2T_m + T_{m+1})/Δx² + ġ/k = 0; convection: h(T∞-T₂) + k(T₁-T₂)/Δx + ġΔx/2 = 0
trap: 🚨 Using half‑thickness for boundary node incorrectly; forgetting that the boundary element volume is Δx/2.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Triangular aluminum fin (k=180 W/m·°C) with L=5 cm, base thickness b=1 cm, width w=1 m, base temperature 200°C, ambient 25°C, h=15 W/m²·°C. Using six equally spaced nodes along the fin, determine (a) nodal temperatures, (b) heat transfer rate, (c) fin efficiency.
solution_steps: Step 1: Δx = L/(M-1) = 0.05/5 = 0.01 m. Use symmetry and fin geometry; heat transfer area varies linearly.
Step 2: For interior node m (1 to 4), energy balance: kA_left (T_{m-1}-T_m)/Δx + kA_right (T_{m+1}-T_m)/Δx + hA_conv (T∞ - T_m) = 0. Areas expressed in terms of x, using tanθ = b/(2L) = 0.1.
Step 3: Derive finite difference equations for m=1..4, and for tip node 5 (with half‑element). Solve system: T₁=198.6, T₂=197.1, T₃=195.7, T₄=194.3, T₅=192.9°C.
Step 4: Heat transfer: Q_fin = Σ h A_conv (T_m - T∞). Compute: Q_fin = 258.4 W.
Step 5: Max heat if entire fin at base: Q_max = h A_total (T_b - T∞) = 15×2×0.05/cosθ×1 ×175 = 263.8 W. Fin efficiency η = Q_fin/Q_max = 0.98.
formula_used: Finite difference with variable cross-section; heat transfer rate summation
trap: 🚨 Using constant cross‑section area; neglecting the taper in area for each node; forgetting to include tip area correctly.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Steady two‑dimensional heat conduction in an L‑shaped solid (k=15 W/m·°C, ġ=2×10⁶ W/m³). Bottom surface at 90°C, left insulated, top convection (h=80 W/m²·°C, T∞=25°C), right surface heat flux q̇_R=5000 W/m². Nodal spacing Δx=Δy=1.2 cm, 15 nodes. Determine temperatures.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify nodes: 0‑4 bottom known, nodes 1‑9 unknown.
Step 2: Write energy balances for each unknown node using appropriate boundary conditions (insulated, convection, heat flux). For interior node 5: (T₄+T₂+T₆+T₁₁ - 4T₅) + ġΔx²/k = 0.
Step 3: For corner node 1 (insulated left, top convection): (hΔx/2)(T∞-T₁) + k(Δy/2)(T₄-T₁)/Δy + k(Δx/2)(T₂-T₁)/Δx + ġ(ΔxΔy/4) = 0.
Step 4: Similar equations for nodes 2,3,4,6,7,8,9. Substitute numerical values.
formula_used: 2D steady energy balance: Σ Q_cond + Σ Q_conv + ġ V = 0
final_answer: Temperatures listed; highest at node 1 (112.1°C), lowest at node 8 (96.3°C)
trap: 🚨 Mis‑identifying half‑size elements at corners; forgetting to include the heat flux boundary condition on the right side; incorrect treatment of insulated boundary as mirror.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Square concrete chimney (k=1.4 W/m·°C) with flow section 20×20 cm, wall thickness 20 cm. Inner surface exposed to hot gases at 300°C, h_i=70 W/m²·°C. Outer surface to ambient at 20°C, h_o=21 W/m²·°C, and sky at 260 K with ε=0.9. Using symmetry and Δx=Δy=10 cm, determine nodal temperatures and heat loss per meter.
solution_steps: Step 1: Use one‑eighth symmetry, nodes 1‑9. Write energy balances considering convection and radiation on outer nodes, and convection on inner nodes.
Step 2: For interior nodes 3,4,5: use 2D interior equation. For boundary nodes, include appropriate convection and radiation terms (absolute temperatures needed for radiation).
Step 3: Set up nonlinear system due to radiation. Iterate using equation solver. Obtain temperatures (in K): T₁=545.7, T₂=529.2, T₃=425.2, T₄=411.2, T₅=362.1, T₆=332.9, T₇=328.1, T₈=313.1, T₉=296.5.

Step 4: Compute heat loss from inner surface: Q = h_i A_i (T_i,avg - T_inner_surface_avg). A_i = 4×0.2×1 = 0.8 m² per meter, T_i,avg = (T₁+T₂)/2 = 264.4°C → Q = 70×0.8×(300-264.4) = 1994 W. Also can compute from outer side with radiation.
Step 5: Heat loss ≈ 1993 W (accounting for both sides).
formula_used: 2D steady energy balance with convection and radiation; nonlinear due to T⁴ terms
final_answer: Nodal temperatures (K) as above; heat loss ≈ 1993 W/m
trap: 🚨 Using Celsius in radiation calculations; neglecting nonlinearity and assuming linear system; incorrect handling of symmetry boundaries.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Large uranium plate (same as Example 5‑1) initially at 200°C, with same conditions, but now transient. Use explicit and implicit methods with three nodes (0,1,2) and Δt=15 s. Determine exposed surface temperature after 2.5 min.
solution_steps: Step 2: Explicit: Node 1 (interior): T₁^{i+1} = T₁^i + τ(T₀^i - 2T₁^i + T₂^i) + ġΔx²/k. Node 2 (convection): T₂^{i+1} = [1-2τ(1+hΔx/k)]T₂^i + 2τ T₁^i + 2τ (hΔx/k)T∞ + 2τ ġΔx²/(2k).
Step 4: March in time with Δt=15 s. After 10 steps (150 s), T₂ ≈ 139.0°C.
Step 5: Implicit: set up equations for each time step using new temperatures; solve simultaneous. After 10 steps, T₂ ≈ 143.9°C.
formula_used: Explicit: T_m^{i+1} = T_m^i + τ(T_{m-1}^i - 2T_m^i + T_{m+1}^i) + ġΔx²/k; implicit uses temperatures at i+1.
trap: 🚨 Using explicit method without checking stability; too large time step causes oscillations. Implicit method avoids stability limit but requires solving equations.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Trombe wall: 1‑ft thick, k=0.40 Btu/h·ft·°F, α=4.78×10⁻⁶ ft²/s. Initial temperature varies linearly from 70°F inside to 30°F outside. Exposed to solar radiation (absorbed 77%) and outdoor air, indoor air at 70°F with h_in=1.8, h_out=0.7 Btu/h·ft²·°F. Use Δx=0.2 ft, explicit method with Δt=900 s. Determine temperatures after 12, 24, 36, 48 h and heat transferred to house.
solution_steps: Step 1: Nodes 0‑5 (0 interior, 5 exterior). τ = αΔt/Δx² = 4.78e-6×900/(0.2²) = 0.10755.

Step 2: Explicit equations: interior nodes (1‑4) use T_m^{i+1} = T_m^i + τ(T_{m-1}^i - 2T_m^i + T_{m+1}^i). Boundary node 0 (convection inside): T₀^{i+1} = T₀^i + 2τ(T₁^i - T₀^i) + 2τ(h_inΔx/k)(T_in - T₀^i). Boundary node 5 (convection + solar): T₅^{i+1} = T₅^i + 2τ(T₄^i - T₅^i) + 2τ(h_outΔx/k)(T_out - T₅^i) + 2τ(α_solar q̇_solar Δx/k).
Step 3: Apply with time‑varying T_out and q̇_solar (3‑h intervals). March in time.
Step 4: Temperatures at 12, 24, 36, 48 h given in Table 5‑5.
Step 5: Heat transfer to house: Q_house = Σ h_in A (T_0^{avg} - T_in) Δt. After 24 h, net = 2483 Btu (loss); after 48 h, net gain of 28,790 Btu.
formula_used: Explicit 1D with time‑dependent boundary conditions; energy balance with convection and solar flux
final_answer: Temperatures and heat transfer given in table and text; after 48 h net gain ~28,790 Btu
trap: 🚨 Using constant ambient and solar when they vary; forgetting to convert time steps to match data intervals; mis‑applying mirror image for interior node.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Same L‑shaped body as Example 5‑3, but now transient. Initially uniform 90°C. At t=0, top surface convects (h=80 W/m²·°C, T∞=25°C), right surface heat flux 5000 W/m². Use Δx=Δy=1.2 cm, explicit method, Δt=10 s. Determine temperature at top corner (node 3) after 1, 3, 5, 10, 60 min.
solution_steps: Step 2: Write explicit energy balances for each node (1‑9) based on geometry and boundary conditions. Example for node 3 (top corner, convection on two sides): T₃^{i+1} = T₃^i + 4τ[ (T₂^i - T₃^i) + (T₆^i - T₃^i) + (hΔx/k)(T∞ - T₃^i) ] + ġΔx²/k.
Step 3: Apply initial condition (all 90°C) and march in time using Δt=10 s.
Step 4: Node 3 temperatures: after 1 min (6 steps) → 100.2°C; 3 min → 105.9°C; 5 min → 106.5°C; 10 min → 106.6°C; 60 min → 106.6°C (steady).
formula_used: Explicit 2D: T_node^{i+1} = T_node^i + τ Σ(T_neighbor^i - T_node^i) + ġΔx²/k
trap: 🚌 Using steady‑state equations; forgetting that corner node has half‑size element and includes convection on two sides; exceeding stability limit (τ must ≤ 0.25).

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Newton's Law of Cooling
desc: The rate of convection heat transfer is proportional to the temperature difference between the surface and the fluid, expressed as q_conv = h (T_s - T_inf) where h is the convection heat transfer coefficient.
formula: q_conv = h (T_s - T_inf) or Q_dot_conv = h A_s (T_s - T_inf)
trap: 🚨 Students often mistake h as a constant; h depends on many factors like flow regime, fluid properties, and geometry, and is not a property of the fluid alone.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Nusselt Number (Nu)
desc: Dimensionless convection heat transfer coefficient representing the enhancement of heat transfer through a fluid layer due to convection relative to pure conduction. Nu = h L_c / k, where L_c is characteristic length.
trap: 🚨 Confusing Nusselt number with Biot number; Nusselt number uses fluid thermal conductivity, while Biot number uses solid thermal conductivity.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Velocity Boundary Layer
desc: Region near a solid surface where fluid velocity changes from zero (no-slip) to nearly the free-stream velocity due to viscous effects. Boundary layer thickness δ is defined as the distance where u = 0.99 u_inf.
formula: δ = 5.0 x / sqrt(Re_x) for laminar flow over flat plate
trap: 🚨 Assuming the boundary layer thickness is constant; it grows with distance from the leading edge.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Prandtl Number (Pr)
desc: Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity, Pr = ν/α = μ C_p / k. Indicates relative thickness of velocity and thermal boundary layers. Pr ~ 1 for gases, <<1 for liquid metals, >>1 for oils.
trap: 🚨 Mistaking that Prandtl number depends on flow conditions; it is a fluid property and depends only on the fluid and its temperature.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Reynolds Number (Re)
desc: Dimensionless parameter representing the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces, used to characterize flow regime. For external flow, Re = ρ V L_c / μ = V L_c / ν. Critical Re for flat plate ≈ 5×10^5.
formula: Re = ρ V L_c / μ = V L_c / ν
trap: 🚨 Using the wrong characteristic length; for flat plate, use distance from leading edge for local Re, or plate length for average Re.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: The flow of oil in a journal bearing can be approximated as parallel flow between two large plates with one plate moving and the other stationary (Couette flow). Consider two large isothermal plates separated by 2-mm-thick oil film. The upper plate moves at a constant velocity of 12 m/s, while the lower plate is stationary. Both plates are maintained at 20°C. (a) Obtain relations for the velocity and temperature distributions in the oil. (b) Determine the maximum temperature in the oil and the heat flux from the oil to each plate.
solution_steps: Step 1: Simplify continuity and x-momentum equations with v=0, u=u(y), and ∂P/∂x=0, giving d²u/dy²=0.
Step 2: Integrate twice and apply no-slip conditions u(0)=0, u(L)=V to get u(y)=V y/L.
Step 3: Energy equation with viscous dissipation reduces to k d²T/dy² + μ (du/dy)² = 0. Substitute du/dy = V/L and integrate twice.
Step 4: Apply boundary conditions T(0)=T(L)=T0 to get T(y) = T0 + (μ V²/(2k)) (y/L - y²/L²).
Step 5: Maximum temperature occurs at y = L/2, T_max = T0 + μ V²/(8k). Compute using μ=0.8 N·s/m², V=12 m/s, k=0.145 W/m·K, L=0.002 m, giving T_max ≈ 119°C.
Step 6: Heat flux at plates: q0 = -k dT/dy|_(y=0) = μ V²/(2L) ≈ 28,800 W/m²; similarly q_L = -μ V²/(2L) (opposite direction).
formula_used: Energy equation with viscous dissipation: k d²T/dy² + μ (du/dy)² = 0; velocity profile: u(y)=V y/L
final_answer: Maximum temperature = 119°C; Heat flux from each plate = 28,800 W/m² (from top plate into oil, from oil into bottom plate)
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to account for viscous dissipation; in high-speed or high-viscosity flows, frictional heating can be significant and must be included in energy equation.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A 2-m × 3-m flat plate is suspended in a room and is subjected to air flow parallel to its surfaces along its 3-m-long side. The free stream temperature and velocity of air are 20°C and 7 m/s. The total drag force acting on the plate is measured to be 0.86 N. Determine the average convection heat transfer coefficient for the plate.
solution_steps: Step 1: Calculate total surface area exposed to flow: both sides, A_s = 2×(2 m × 3 m) = 12 m².

Step 2: Determine average friction coefficient from drag force: F_f = C_f A_s (ρ V²/2) → C_f = 2F_f/(ρ V² A_s). Using ρ=1.204 kg/m³ (air at 20°C), V=7 m/s, compute C_f = 2×0.86/(1.204×49×12) = 0.00243.
Step 3: Apply modified Reynolds analogy (Chilton-Colburn) for Pr ≈ 0.73: C_f/2 = St Pr^{2/3}, where St = h/(ρ V C_p).
Step 4: Solve for h: h = (C_f/2) ρ V C_p / Pr^{2/3}.
formula_used: C_f/2 = St Pr^{2/3}, St = h/(ρ V C_p)
final_answer: Average convection heat transfer coefficient ≈ 12.7 W/m²·°C
trap: 🚨 Using only one side area for drag force; drag force acts on both sides of the plate if flow is parallel to both surfaces. Also, the analogy is valid for turbulent flow and approximately for laminar if no pressure gradient.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Reynolds and Chilton-Colburn Analogies
desc: Relations linking momentum transfer (friction) and heat transfer (convection) in boundary layers. Reynolds analogy: for Pr=1, C_f/2 = St. Chilton-Colburn analogy extends to 0.6<Pr<60: C_f/2 = St Pr^{2/3} = j_H, where j_H is Colburn j-factor.
formula: C_f/2 = St Pr^{2/3}, where St = Nu/(Re Pr) = h/(ρ V C_p)
trap: 🚨 Applying Reynolds analogy directly for fluids with Pr not equal to 1 without Prandtl number correction; Chilton-Colburn analogy must be used for most gases and liquids.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Drag and Pressure Drag
desc: Drag is the force a flowing fluid exerts on a body in the flow direction. Skin friction drag is due to wall shear stress; pressure drag (form drag) is due to pressure differences. For a flat plate parallel to flow, pressure drag is zero.
trap: 🚨 Confusing frontal area (for blunt bodies) with surface area (for flat plates) when computing drag coefficient. Also, assuming pressure drag is always negligible.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Film Temperature
desc: To account for property variation across the boundary layer, fluid properties are evaluated at the film temperature, the arithmetic mean of surface and free‑stream temperatures: T_f = (T_s + T_∞)/2.
trap: 🚨 Using free‑stream properties directly when property variation is significant, especially for large temperature differences.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Flat Plate Laminar Flow Correlations
desc: For laminar flow over a flat plate (Re_x < 5×10⁵), local friction and Nusselt numbers: C_f,x = 0.664/√Re_x, Nu_x = 0.332 Re_x^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}. Average values: C_f = 1.328/√Re_L, Nu = 0.664 Re_L^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}.
trap: 🚌 Using the local correlation to compute total heat transfer without integrating; forgetting that the average Nusselt number is twice the local value at x = L for laminar flow.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Flat Plate Turbulent Flow Correlations
desc: For turbulent flow (5×10⁵ < Re_x < 10⁷) over a smooth flat plate: local C_f,x = 0.0592/Re_x^{1/5}, Nu_x = 0.0296 Re_x^{4/5} Pr^{1/3}. Average for fully turbulent: C_f = 0.074/Re_L^{1/5}, Nu = 0.037 Re_L^{4/5} Pr^{1/3}.
trap: 🚨 Applying turbulent correlations when the boundary layer is laminar or mixed; ignoring the effect of surface roughness.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Mixed Boundary Layer over Flat Plate
desc: When the plate is long enough for transition to occur, the average Nusselt number is computed by integrating laminar up to x_cr and turbulent thereafter. For Re_cr = 5×10⁵, Nu = (0.037 Re_L^{4/5} – 871) Pr^{1/3}.
trap: 🚌 Disregarding the laminar portion entirely when Re_L > Re_cr, which overestimates heat transfer.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Unheated Starting Length
desc: When heating begins at a distance ξ from the leading edge, the thermal boundary layer starts at x = ξ. The local Nusselt number for laminar flow is Nu_x = 0.332 Re_x^{1/2} Pr^{1/3} / [1 – (ξ/x)^{3/4}]^{1/3}. The average heat transfer coefficient over the heated section is h = 2h_{x=L} [1 – (ξ/L)^{3/4}] / (1 – ξ/L).
trap: 🚨 Using the standard flat plate correlation without accounting for the unheated length, which overestimates heat transfer.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Cross Flow over Cylinder
desc: Flow separation and wake affect heat transfer. The Churchill‑Bernstein correlation for average Nusselt number: Nu = 0.3 + (0.62 Re^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}) / [1 + (0.4/Pr)^{2/3}]^{1/4} × [1 + (Re/282000)^{5/8}]^{4/5}, valid for Re·Pr > 0.2.
trap: 🚨 Using a single‑cylinder correlation for tube banks; forgetting to evaluate properties at film temperature.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Cross Flow over Sphere
desc: Whitaker correlation for spheres: Nu = 2 + [0.4 Re^{1/2} + 0.06 Re^{2/3}] Pr^{0.4} (μ_∞/μ_s)^{1/4}, valid for 3.5 < Re < 8×10⁴, 0.7 < Pr < 380.
trap: 🚨 Neglecting the viscosity ratio correction, which is important for large temperature differences; using wrong characteristic length.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Tube Banks – Inline and Staggered
desc: Heat transfer and pressure drop depend on tube arrangement. Maximum velocity occurs at minimum flow area: for inline V_max = V_∞ S_T/(S_T – D); for staggered, if diagonal spacing > (S_T+D)/2, the same formula holds, otherwise V_max = V_∞ S_T / [2(S_D – D)].
formula: V_max = V_∞ S_T/(S_T - D) (inline); V_max = V_∞ S_T/[2(S_D - D)] (staggered, if S_D < (S_T+D)/2)
trap: 🚌 Using approach velocity instead of maximum velocity for Reynolds number, underestimating Re and thus heat transfer.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Zukauskas Correlation for Tube Banks
desc: For tube banks with NL ≥ 16, Nu_D = C Re_D^m Pr^{0.36} (Pr/Pr_s)^{0.25}, where constants C, m depend on Re range (see Table 7‑2). For fewer rows, multiply by a correction factor F (<1).
trap: 🚨 Using the correlation for NL < 16 without the correction factor; evaluating properties at wrong temperature.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Pressure Drop in Tube Banks
desc: Pressure drop ΔP = N_L f (ρ V_max²/2) χ, where f is the friction factor from graphs (Fig. 7‑27) and χ is a correction factor for non‑square arrangements. It accounts for the flow resistance offered by the tube bundle.
trap: 🚌 Using the number of tubes instead of number of rows; forgetting the correction factor for non‑square pitch.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: R‑value of Insulation
desc: R‑value is the thermal resistance per unit area: R = L/k for flat insulation (in h·ft²·°F/Btu or m²·°C/W). Higher R‑value means better insulation. Pipe insulation R‑value = (ln(r₂/r₁))/(2πk) per unit length.
trap: 🚨 Mixing English and SI units; using R‑value without proper units (e.g., R‑19 means 19 h·ft²·°F/Btu).

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Engine oil at 60°C flows over a 5‑m‑long flat plate whose temperature is 20°C with a velocity of 2 m/s. Determine the total drag force and the rate of heat transfer per unit width of the entire plate. (Properties at film temperature 40°C: ρ=876 kg/m³, ν=242×10⁻⁶ m²/s, k=0.144 W/m·°C, Pr=2870.)
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute Re_L = V L/ν = 2×5 / (242×10⁻⁶) = 4.13×10⁴ < 5×10⁵ → laminar.
Step 2: Average friction coefficient C_f = 1.328/√Re_L = 1.328/√(4.13×10⁴) = 0.0207.
Step 3: Drag force per unit width: F_D = C_f (ρ V²/2) A = 0.0207 × (876×2²/2) × (5×1) = 181 N.
final_answer: Drag force = 181 N per meter width; heat transfer = 11,040 W per meter width
trap: 🚨 Using turbulent correlations for laminar flow; mis‑calculating characteristic length for flat plate.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Air at 83.4 kPa and 20°C flows at 8 m/s over a 1.5 m × 6 m flat plate at 140°C. Determine the heat transfer rate for flow parallel to (a) the 6‑m side and (b) the 1.5‑m side.
solution_steps: Step 1: Film temperature T_f = (140+20)/2 = 80°C. Air properties at 1 atm: k=0.02953 W/m·°C, ν=2.097×10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr=0.7154. At 83.4 kPa, ν = ν_1atm×(101.3/83.4) = 2.548×10⁻⁵ m²/s.
trap: 🚌 Using approach velocity without correcting ν for pressure; ignoring the effect of flow orientation on Re.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A 10‑cm‑diameter steam pipe at 110°C is in air at 10°C with wind at 8 m/s. Determine the heat loss per unit length. (Properties at film temperature 60°C: k=0.02808 W/m·°C, ν=1.896×10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr=0.7202.)
trap: 🚌 Using a simpler power‑law correlation without checking the Re range; forgetting to evaluate properties at film temperature.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A 25‑cm‑diameter stainless steel ball (ρ=8055 kg/m³, Cp=480 J/kg·°C) at 300°C is cooled by air at 25°C, 1 atm, velocity 3 m/s. Determine the average convection coefficient and the cooling time to reach 200°C. (Properties: at T∞=25°C: k=0.02551, ν=1.562×10⁻⁵, Pr=0.7296, μ=1.849×10⁻⁵; at T_s,avg=250°C: μ_s=2.76×10⁻⁵.)
solution_steps: Step 2: Whitaker correlation: Nu = 2 + [0.4 Re^{1/2} + 0.06 Re^{2/3}] Pr^{0.4} (μ/μ_s)^{1/4}.
Step 7: Surface area A = πD²=0.1963 m². Average heat rate Q_avg = h A (T_avg – T∞) = 13.8×0.1963×(250-25)=610 W.
Step 8: Mass m = ρ V = 8055×(π/6)×0.25³ = 65.9 kg. Heat to remove Q_total = m Cp ΔT = 65.9×480×(300-200)=3.163×10⁶ J.
Step 9: Time t = Q_total / Q_avg = 3.163e6/610 = 5185 s ≈ 1.44 h.
trap: 🚨 Using average surface temperature for film temperature instead of free‑stream temperature; forgetting viscosity ratio correction.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Air at 20°C, 1 atm, flows at 4.5 m/s over an in‑line tube bank. Tubes: D=1.5 cm, ST=SL=5 cm, 6 rows × 10 tubes per row, Ts=120°C. Determine the heat transfer rate per unit tube length and pressure drop.
solution_steps: Step 1: Properties at mean temperature (assume 60°C): k=0.02808, ν=2.008×10⁻⁵, Pr=0.7202, ρ=1.06 kg/m³. At Ts=120°C, Pr_s=0.7073.
Step 2: Maximum velocity V_max = V_∞ ST/(ST-D) = 4.5×0.05/(0.05-0.015)=6.43 m/s.
Step 5: Correction for NL=6: F=0.945 (Table 7‑3) → Nu_NL = 0.945×52.2 = 49.3.
Step 7: Heat transfer area per unit length: A = N π D L = 60×π×0.015×1 = 2.827 m². Mass flow rate ṁ = ρ V_∞ (NT ST L) = 1.06×4.5×(10×0.05×1)=2.709 kg/s.
Step 10: Pressure drop: From Fig. 7‑27a, for SL/D=3.33, f=0.16, χ=1. ΔP = NL f (ρ V_max²/2) = 6×0.16×(1.06×6.43²/2) = 21 Pa.
formula_used: Nu = 0.27 Re^{0.63} Pr^{0.36} (Pr/Pr_s)^{0.25}; Te = Ts – (Ts–Ti) exp(–hA/(ṁCp)); ΔP = NL f ρ V_max²/2
final_answer: Heat transfer rate = 24.9 kW per meter length; pressure drop = 21 Pa
trap: 🚌 Using approach velocity for Re; neglecting correction factor for number of rows; mis‑reading friction factor chart.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Hydraulic Diameter (Dh)
desc: A characteristic length for non‑circular ducts defined as four times the cross‑sectional area divided by the wetted perimeter. For a circular tube it reduces to the ordinary diameter.
trap: 🚨 Using the hydraulic diameter in laminar flow correlations for non‑circular ducts is only approximate; for accurate results, use tabulated Nusselt numbers and friction factors for the specific cross‑section.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Mean Velocity and Mean Temperature
desc: Mean velocity (Vm) is the average axial velocity based on mass flow rate; mean temperature (Tm) is the bulk temperature that would transport the same energy if the fluid were uniform at that temperature.
formula: Vm = (1/Ac) ∫ u dAc ; Tm = (1/(Vm Ac)) ∫ u T dAc
trap: 🚨 Confusing mean temperature with the arithmetic average of inlet and exit temperatures when evaluating properties; properties should be evaluated at the bulk mean temperature Tb = (Ti + Te)/2 for fully developed flow.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Reynolds Number for Internal Flow
desc: Dimensionless parameter distinguishing laminar (Re < 2300), transitional (2300–4000), and turbulent (Re > 4000) flow regimes in pipes. For non‑circular ducts, use hydraulic diameter.
formula: Re = (ρ Vm D) / μ = Vm D / ν
trap: 🚨 Using the diameter of a non‑circular duct directly instead of hydraulic diameter; also using local velocity instead of mean velocity.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Entry Lengths for Internal Flow
desc: Hydrodynamic entry length Lh is the distance from inlet to where velocity profile becomes fully developed; thermal entry length Lt is similar for temperature profile. For laminar flow, Lh ~ 0.05 Re D, Lt ~ 0.05 Re Pr D; for turbulent flow, both are about 10 diameters.
formula: Lh,lam = 0.05 Re D ; Lt,lam = 0.05 Re Pr D ; Lh,turb ≈ Lt,turb ≈ 10 D
trap: 🚨 Ignoring entry lengths in short tubes; the Nusselt number and friction factor are higher in the entrance region, so using fully developed values for short tubes underestimates heat transfer and pressure drop.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Fully Developed Laminar Flow in Circular Tube
desc: Velocity profile is parabolic; mean velocity is half the centerline velocity. Friction factor f = 64/Re, independent of surface roughness. Nusselt number is constant: Nu = 4.36 for uniform heat flux, Nu = 3.66 for uniform wall temperature.
trap: 🚌 Using the constant Nusselt numbers for developing flow or for non‑circular ducts without adjustment; also forgetting to use hydraulic diameter for non‑circular laminar flow.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Pressure Drop and Friction Factor
desc: Pressure drop ΔP in a horizontal pipe of length L is related to the Darcy friction factor f by ΔP = f (L/D) (ρ Vm²/2). Pumping power = V̇ ΔP.
formula: ΔP = f (L/D) (ρ Vm²/2) ; Ẇpump = V̇ ΔP
trap: 🚨 Confusing Darcy friction factor f with Fanning friction factor Cf = f/4; always verify which definition is used in correlations.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Developing Laminar Flow – Constant Wall Temperature
desc: For thermally developing laminar flow in a circular tube with constant surface temperature, the average Nusselt number can be approximated by the Edwards or Sieder–Tate correlations. The Nusselt number is higher than the fully developed value of 3.66.
trap: 🚨 Applying these correlations when the flow is not hydrodynamically developed; the first equation assumes developed velocity profile at the start of heating, while the second is for combined entry.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Turbulent Flow in Smooth Tubes – Dittus‑Boelter
desc: Empirical correlation for fully developed turbulent flow (Re > 10,000, 0.7 < Pr < 160) in smooth tubes. Nusselt number = 0.023 Re^0.8 Pr^n with n = 0.4 for heating and n = 0.3 for cooling.
trap: 🚨 Using this correlation outside its range or for rough tubes; also forgetting to evaluate properties at bulk mean temperature.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Turbulent Flow – Gnielinski Correlation
desc: Accurate correlation for fully developed turbulent flow in smooth tubes (3000 < Re < 5×10^6, 0.5 < Pr < 2000). Nusselt number = (f/8)(Re-1000)Pr / [1 + 12.7 (f/8)^0.5 (Pr^(2/3)-1)], with f from Petukhov.
trap: 🚨 Applying Gnielinski for Prandtl numbers below 0.5 or above 2000; also using it for transitional flow without caution.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Colebrook Equation and Moody Chart
desc: Implicit relation for Darcy friction factor f in turbulent pipe flow, accounting for relative roughness ε/D. The Moody chart is a graphical representation. For smooth tubes, f = 0.184 Re^(-0.2) for Re up to 10^5, or Petukhov’s explicit formula.
trap: 🚨 Using the smooth‑tube friction factor for rough pipes; roughness significantly increases f and Nusselt number in turbulent flow.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)
desc: Exact average temperature difference for a tube with constant surface temperature, accounting for exponential decay of the fluid temperature along the tube.
formula: ΔT_ln = (ΔTi - ΔTe) / ln(ΔTi/ΔTe) ; ΔTi = Ts - Ti, ΔTe = Ts - Te
trap: 🚨 Using arithmetic mean temperature difference when ΔTe and ΔTi differ by more than 40%, which can cause significant error.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Water enters a 2.5‑cm‑internal‑diameter thin copper tube of a heat exchanger at 15°C at a rate of 0.3 kg/s, and is heated by steam condensing outside at 120°C. If the average heat transfer coefficient is 800 W/m²·°C, determine the length of the tube required in order to heat the water to 115°C.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute heat transfer rate using Q̇ = ṁ Cp (Te – Ti). With water properties at bulk mean temperature 65°C, Cp = 4187 J/kg·K. Q̇ = 0.3 × 4187 × (115 – 15) = 125,610 W.
Step 2: Calculate log mean temperature difference: ΔTi = Ts – Ti = 120 – 15 = 105°C, ΔTe = Ts – Te = 120 – 115 = 5°C. ΔT_ln = (105 – 5) / ln(105/5) = 32.85°C.
Step 3: Use Q̇ = h A_s ΔT_ln to find surface area: A_s = Q̇ / (h ΔT_ln) = 125,610 / (800 × 32.85) = 4.78 m².
Step 4: Relate area to length: A_s = π D L → L = A_s / (π D) = 4.78 / (π × 0.025) = 60.9 m ≈ 61 m.
formula_used: Q̇ = ṁ Cp (Te – Ti) ; ΔT_ln = (ΔTi – ΔTe) / ln(ΔTi/ΔTe) ; Q̇ = h A_s ΔT_ln ; A_s = π D L
trap: 🚨 Using the arithmetic mean temperature difference (55°C) instead of the LMTD would give a length of only 36 m, a gross underestimate.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Water at 40°F (ρ = 62.42 lbm/ft³, μ = 3.74 lbm/ft·h) is flowing in a 0.15‑in‑diameter, 30‑ft‑long pipe steadily at an average velocity of 3 ft/s. Determine the pressure drop and the pumping power requirement.
solution_steps: Step 1: Calculate Reynolds number: convert μ to lbm/ft·s: μ = 3.74/3600 = 0.001039 lbm/ft·s. Re = ρ V D / μ = 62.42 × 3 × (0.15/12) / 0.001039 ≈ 1803 → laminar.
Step 2: Friction factor f = 64/Re = 64/1803 = 0.0355.
Step 4: Volume flow rate V̇ = V × A_c = 3 × (π×(0.15/12)²/4) = 0.000236 ft³/s. Pumping power = V̇ ΔP = 0.000236 × 930 = 0.22 ft·lbf/s = 0.30 W.
formula_used: Re = ρ V D / μ ; f = 64/Re ; ΔP = f (L/D) (ρ V²/2) ; Ẇpump = V̇ ΔP
final_answer: Pressure drop = 6.46 psi, Pumping power = 0.30 W
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert viscosity units to consistent lbm/ft·s, or using the wrong diameter (0.15 in = 0.0125 ft).

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Oil at 20°C flows in a 30‑cm‑diameter pipeline at 2 m/s. A 200‑m section passes through icy water at 0°C; pipe surface is at 0°C. Determine (a) exit oil temperature, (b) rate of heat transfer, (c) pumping power.
solution_steps: Step 1: Evaluate properties at inlet (later refined). ρ = 888 kg/m³, μ = 901×10⁻⁶ Pa·s, k = 0.145 W/m·K, Cp = 1880 J/kg·K, Pr = 10,400. Re = V D / ν = 2×0.3 / (901×10⁻⁶) = 666 → laminar.
Step 4: Exit temperature: Te = Ts – (Ts – Ti) exp(–h A_s/(ṁ Cp)) = 0 – (0 – 20) exp(–18.0×188.5/(125.5×1880)) = 19.71°C.
formula_used: Re = V D / ν ; Nu_entry = 3.66 + 0.065 (D/L) Re Pr / [1 + 0.04((D/L) Re Pr)^(2/3)] ; h = Nu k/D ; Te = Ts – (Ts – Ti)exp(–hA_s/(ṁ Cp)) ; Q̇ = ṁ Cp (Ti – Te) ; ΔP = f (L/D)(ρ V²/2) ; Ẇpump = ṁ ΔP / ρ
trap: 🚨 Assuming fully developed laminar Nusselt number (3.66) would greatly underpredict h and thus overpredict Te, and neglecting the large Prandtl number effect on thermal entry length.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Water at 60°F (ρ = 62.36 lbm/ft³, μ = 2.713 lbm/ft·h) flows in a 2‑in‑diameter stainless steel pipe at 0.2 ft³/s. Determine pressure drop and pumping power for a 200‑ft section.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute mean velocity: V = V̇ / A_c = 0.2 / (π×(2/12)²/4) = 9.17 ft/s.
Step 3: Relative roughness for stainless steel: ε = 0.000007 ft (Table 8‑3). ε/D = 0.000007/(2/12) = 0.000042.
Step 6: Pumping power = V̇ ΔP = 0.2×1700 = 340 ft·lbf/s = 461 W.
formula_used: Re = ρ V D / μ ; Haaland: 1/√f = –1.8 log₁₀[ (ε/D)/3.7 + (6.9/Re)^1.11 ] ; ΔP = f (L/D)(ρ V²/2) ; Ẇpump = V̇ ΔP
trap: 🚌 Using the smooth‑pipe friction factor would give f = 0.0171, almost identical here because the pipe is very smooth; but for rougher pipes the difference is significant.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Water is heated from 15°C to 65°C in a 3‑cm‑ID, 5‑m‑long tube with uniform surface heat flux. Flow rate = 10 L/min. Determine the power rating of the electric heater and the inner surface temperature at the exit.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute mass flow rate: V̇ = 10 L/min = 0.01 m³/min = 0.0001667 m³/s. ρ at bulk mean (40°C) = 992.1 kg/m³, so ṁ = 992.1×0.0001667 = 0.1654 kg/s.
Step 2: Heat required: Q̇ = ṁ Cp (Te – Ti) = 0.1654×4179×(65 – 15) = 34,560 W = 34.6 kW. This is the heater power.
Step 3: Surface heat flux: A_s = π D L = π×0.03×5 = 0.4712 m². q̇s = Q̇ / A_s = 34,560/0.4712 = 73,400 W/m².
Step 6: h = Nu k/D = 69.5×0.631/0.03 = 1462 W/m²·K. Exit surface temperature: Ts = Tm + q̇s/h = 65 + 73,400/1462 = 115°C.
formula_used: Q̇ = ṁ Cp (Te – Ti) ; q̇s = Q̇ / A_s ; Re = V D / ν ; Nu = 0.023 Re^0.8 Pr^0.4 ; h = Nu k/D ; Ts = Tm + q̇s/h
final_answer: Heater power = 34.6 kW; Exit inner surface temperature = 115°C
trap: 🚨 Using Dittus‑Boelter with n=0.3 (cooling) for heating would give a lower Nusselt number and thus a higher surface temperature prediction.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Hot air at 1 atm, 80°C enters an 8‑m‑long square duct (0.2 m × 0.2 m) at 0.15 m³/s. Duct surface is nearly isothermal at 60°C. Determine exit temperature and heat loss rate.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute hydraulic diameter: Dh = 4Ac/p = 4×0.2²/(4×0.2) = 0.2 m (square duct).
Step 2: Mean velocity: V = V̇ / Ac = 0.15 / 0.04 = 3.75 m/s.
Step 3: Air properties at inlet (80°C): ρ = 0.9994 kg/m³, ν = 2.097×10⁻⁵ m²/s, k = 0.02953 W/m·K, Pr = 0.7154, Cp = 1008 J/kg·K.
Step 8: Exit temperature: Te = Ts – (Ts – Ti) exp(–h A_s/(ṁ Cp)) = 60 – (60–80) exp(–13.5×6.4/(0.1499×1008)) = 71.3°C.
formula_used: Dh = 4Ac/p ; Re = V Dh / ν ; Nu = 0.023 Re^0.8 Pr^0.3 ; h = Nu k/Dh ; Te = Ts – (Ts – Ti) exp(–h A_s/(ṁ Cp))
trap: 🚨 Using the side length as characteristic length for a square duct instead of hydraulic diameter; also using the heating exponent (0.4) for cooling would give a slightly different Nu.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Physical Mechanism of Natural Convection
desc: Natural convection is driven by buoyancy forces due to density differences caused by temperature variations in a gravitational field. Heated fluid becomes less dense and rises, while cooler fluid descends, creating natural convection currents that enhance heat transfer.
trap: 🚨 Confusing natural convection with forced convection; neglecting natural convection in still fluids or assuming it is negligible without checking Gr/Re².

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Volume Expansion Coefficient
desc: The volume expansion coefficient β measures the change in density with temperature at constant pressure. For an ideal gas, β = 1/T (absolute temperature). It is used to relate density differences to temperature differences in the buoyancy term.
formula: β = -1/ρ (∂ρ/∂T)_P ≈ (ρ∞ - ρ)/(ρ (T - T∞)); for ideal gas: β = 1/T
trap: 🚨 Using Celsius instead of Kelvin for β in ideal gases; forgetting that β is temperature‑dependent for real fluids.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Grashof Number
desc: The Grashof number Gr = g β (Ts - T∞) Lc³ / ν² represents the ratio of buoyancy force to viscous force in natural convection. It determines whether flow is laminar or turbulent. For vertical plates, transition occurs around Gr ≈ 10⁹.
formula: Gr = g β (Ts - T∞) Lc³ / ν²
trap: 🚨 Using wrong characteristic length; forgetting that Gr is the natural convection analog of Re in forced convection.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Rayleigh Number
desc: The Rayleigh number Ra = Gr Pr combines buoyancy and thermal diffusion effects. It is the primary parameter in natural convection correlations. Flow regimes and Nusselt numbers are expressed as Ra^n.
formula: Ra = Gr Pr = g β (Ts - T∞) Lc³ Pr / ν²
trap: 🚌 Using Ra without proper Prandtl number evaluation; confusing with Reynolds number.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Nusselt Number Correlations for Various Geometries
desc: Natural convection Nusselt numbers are correlated as Nu = C Ra^n, with constants C and n depending on geometry and flow regime. Table 9-1 provides correlations for vertical plates, horizontal plates, cylinders, spheres, and finned surfaces.
trap: 🚨 Using the wrong characteristic length for each geometry; applying a correlation outside its Ra range.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Churchill‑Chu Correlation for Vertical Plate
desc: For an isothermal vertical plate, the average Nusselt number over the entire plate is given by Nu = [0.825 + 0.387 Ra^{1/6} / (1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16})^{8/27}]², valid for all Ra (laminar and turbulent).
trap: 🚌 Using the simplified laminar or turbulent correlations when Ra falls in the mixed regime; forgetting that Ra uses height L.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Optimum Fin Spacing for Natural Convection Heat Sinks
desc: For vertical isothermal parallel plates (fins), there is an optimum spacing S_opt that maximizes heat transfer. It is given by S_opt = 2.714 (L / Ra_S^{1/4}) = 2.714 (L / (Ra_L (S/L)^3)^{1/4}). The Nusselt number at optimum spacing is constant Nu_S = 1.307.
trap: 🚨 Using S as characteristic length incorrectly; forgetting to evaluate properties at film temperature.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Natural Convection in Enclosures
desc: In enclosures, fluid motion is driven by buoyancy. The Nusselt number is defined based on the gap thickness Lc. For horizontal enclosures heated from below, Nu > 1 (convection) above Ra ≈ 1708; when heated from above, Nu = 1 (pure conduction). Correlations exist for various orientations and aspect ratios.
formula: Nu = h Lc / k; effective thermal conductivity k_eff = Nu k
trap: 🚨 Using enclosure height instead of gap thickness as Lc; assuming Nu=1 for all horizontal enclosures.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Combined Natural and Forced Convection
desc: The parameter Gr/Re² indicates the relative importance: if Gr/Re² << 0.1, forced convection dominates; if Gr/Re² >> 10, natural convection dominates; otherwise both are significant. For assisting flow, Nu_combined = (Nu_forced^n ± Nu_natural^n)^{1/n} with n ≈ 3.
trap: 🚨 Ignoring natural convection when Gr/Re² is not negligible, especially at low velocities.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A 6‑m‑long section of an 8‑cm‑diameter horizontal hot water pipe passes through a large room at 20°C. The outer surface temperature of the pipe is 70°C. Determine the rate of heat loss from the pipe by natural convection.
solution_steps: Step 1: Film temperature T_f = (70+20)/2 = 45°C. Properties of air at 45°C: k=0.02699 W/m·°C, ν=1.749×10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr=0.7241, β=1/(45+273)=1/318 K⁻¹.
Step 2: Rayleigh number based on diameter D=0.08 m: Ra_D = g β (Ts-T∞) D³ Pr / ν² = 9.81×(1/318)×50×0.08³×0.7241/(1.749e-5)² = 1.869×10⁶.
Step 4: h = Nu k/D = 17.40×0.02699/0.08 = 5.869 W/m²·°C. Surface area per meter: A = π D L = π×0.08×1 = 0.2513 m²/m. For L=6 m, total A=1.508 m².
trap: 🚨 Using the wrong characteristic length; evaluating properties at wrong temperature; neglecting that radiation also contributes.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A 0.6 m × 0.6 m thin square plate at 90°C is placed in a room at 30°C. One side is insulated. Determine the rate of heat transfer by natural convection if the plate is (a) vertical, (b) horizontal with hot surface facing up, (c) horizontal with hot surface facing down.
solution_steps: Step 1: Film temperature T_f = (90+30)/2 = 60°C. Air properties: k=0.02808 W/m·°C, ν=1.896×10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr=0.7202, β=1/(60+273)=1/333 K⁻¹.
final_answer: Vertical: 115 W; Horizontal up: 128 W; Horizontal down: 64.2 W
trap: 🚨 Using the same characteristic length for horizontal as for vertical; mixing up correlations for up and down orientations.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A 12‑cm‑wide, 18‑cm‑high vertical hot surface at 80°C is to be cooled by a heat sink with equally spaced rectangular fins (t=0.1 cm, H=2.4 cm, L=18 cm). Ambient air at 30°C. Determine the optimum fin spacing and the rate of heat transfer by natural convection from the heat sink if the base temperature is 80°C.
solution_steps: Step 1: Film temperature T_f = (80+30)/2 = 55°C. Air properties: k=0.02772 W/m·°C, ν=1.846×10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr=0.7215, β=1/(55+273)=1/328 K⁻¹.
Step 4: Number of fins n ≈ W/(S_opt + t) = 0.12/(0.00745+0.001) ≈ 14.2 → take n=15.

Step 5: For optimum spacing, Nu_S = 1.307. h = Nu_S k / S_opt = 1.307×0.02772/0.00745 = 4.86 W/m²·°C? Wait, careful: In the text they used a different approach and got h = 0.2012 W/m²·°C? Let's re-check: Actually the text says h = Nu k/S_opt = 1.307×0.02772/0.00745 ≈ 4.86 W/m²·°C. But they computed h = 0.2012? Possibly they used different Nu definition? In the example, they used Nu = 1.307 and then h = k/S_opt * Nu = 0.02772/0.00745 * 1.307 = 4.86. Then Q = h * (2nLH) * ΔT = 4.86 * (2*15*0.18*0.024) * 50 = 4.86 * 0.1296 * 50 = 31.5 W. But their final answer was 1.30 W. There's inconsistency. The example in the text seems to have a different h calculation: they wrote h = 0.2012 W/m²·°C. Possibly they used a different formula (maybe they used Nu = 1.307 but then divided by something else). Let's check the text:

Actually that gives 4.86, not 0.2012. The 0.2012 appears to be 1.307 * 0.02772 / 0.18? That would be 0.2012. Possibly they used L instead of S_opt? I'll stick to the given numbers. I'll use the exact numbers from the example: They got h = 0.2012, Q = 1.30 W. I'll replicate those numbers.

Step 6: They computed: h = 1.307 k/S_opt = 1.307 * 0.02772 / 0.00745 = 4.86? No, that gives 4.86. Wait they wrote: h = 1.307 k / S_opt = 1.307 * 0.02772 / 0.00745 = 4.86. Then Q = h * (2nLH) ΔT = 4.86 * (2*15*0.18*0.024) * 50 = 4.86 * 0.1296 * 50 = 31.5 W. But the text says 1.30 W. There is a factor of 24 difference. Possibly they used the formula h = 1.307 k / L instead? That would be 1.307*0.02772/0.18 = 0.2012. So they used L as characteristic length in Nu definition? But Nu = hS/k, so if Nu = 1.307, then h = 1.307 k/S. To get h = 0.2012, they would need S = 1.307*0.02772/0.2012 = 0.18 m, which is L. So they inadvertently used L instead of S. But the example in the textbook likely contains an error. I'll present the solution as given in the text: S_opt = 7.45 mm, n=15, h=0.2012 W/m²·°C, Q=1.30 W. I'll note the discrepancy in the trap.
formula_used: S_opt = 2.714 L / Ra_L^{1/4}; Nu_S = 1.307; h = Nu_S k / S_opt; Q = h * (2 n L H) ΔT
final_answer: Optimum spacing = 7.45 mm; heat transfer rate = 1.30 W
trap: 🚨 Potential confusion in the example: the heat transfer coefficient calculated using Nu_S = 1.307 should be h = Nu_S k / S_opt, not h = Nu_S k / L. The example seems to have used L instead, leading to an inconsistency. Always verify the definition of the Nusselt number used in the correlation.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A vertical double‑pane window (0.8 m high, 2 m wide) has a 2‑cm air gap at atmospheric pressure. The glass surface temperatures across the gap are 12°C and 2°C. Determine the rate of heat transfer through the window by natural convection (neglect radiation).
solution_steps: Step 1: Average temperature T_ave = (12+2)/2 = 7°C. Air properties at 7°C: k=0.02416 W/m·°C, ν=1.399×10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr=0.7344, β=1/(7+273)=1/280 K⁻¹.
Step 2: Gap thickness L = 0.02 m, height H = 0.8 m. Ra_L = g β ΔT L³ Pr / ν² = 9.81×(1/280)×10×0.02³×0.7344/(1.399e-5)² = 1.051×10⁴.
Step 5: Using the more accurate correlation in the text they got Nu=1.401, Q=27.1 W. Acceptable.
trap: 🚨 Using the wrong characteristic length (height instead of gap thickness); forgetting to account for aspect ratio in the correlation.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Two concentric spheres of diameters Di=20 cm and Do=30 cm are separated by air at 1 atm. The surfaces are at Ti=320 K and To=280 K. Determine the rate of heat transfer by natural convection.
solution_steps: Step 1: Average temperature T_ave = (320+280)/2 = 300 K. Air properties: k=0.02566 W/m·°C, ν=1.58×10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr=0.729, β=1/300 K⁻¹.
Step 2: Characteristic length L_c = (Do-Di)/2 = (0.3-0.2)/2 = 0.05 m. Rayleigh number Ra_L = g β ΔT L_c³ Pr / ν² = 9.81×(1/300)×40×0.05³×0.729/(1.58e-5)² = 4.78×10⁵.

Step 5: k_eff = 2.78×0.02566 = 0.0713 W/m·°C. Q = 4π k_eff (Di Do / L_c) (Ti-To) = 4π×0.0713×(0.2×0.3/0.05)×40 = 4π×0.0713×1.2×40 = 4π×3.4224 = 43.0 W. Wait text gives 16.7 W. I need to recalc: They used a different formulation. Actually their formula: Q = k_eff Di Do / L_c (Ti-To) times 2π? No, for spheres Q = 4π k_eff (Ti-To) / (1/ri - 1/ro) = 4π k_eff ri ro / (ro-ri) (Ti-To). That is 4π k_eff (Di Do)/(2L_c) (Ti-To)? Actually (ro-ri)=L_c. ri = Di/2, ro = Do/2, so 4π ri ro / (ro-ri) = 4π (Di/2)(Do/2)/L_c = π Di Do / L_c. So Q = π k_eff Di Do / L_c (Ti-To). That's a factor of π, not 4π. Their final formula: Q = k_eff (Di Do)/L_c (Ti-To) * π? I'll use the correct formula: Q = (2π k_eff) / (1/ri - 1/ro) (Ti-To)?? Actually for conduction through a spherical shell: Q = 4π k (Ti-To) / (1/ri - 1/ro) = 4π k ri ro (Ti-To)/(ro-ri). So Q = 4π k (ri ro / L_c) ΔT. Since ri = Di/2, ro = Do/2, then Q = 4π k (Di Do / 4) / L_c ΔT = π k Di Do / L_c ΔT. So Q = π k_eff Di Do / L_c ΔT. Using k_eff=0.0713, Di=0.2, Do=0.3, L_c=0.05, ΔT=40 → Q = π×0.0713×0.2×0.3/0.05×40 = π×0.0713×1.2×40 = π×3.4224 = 10.75 W. Still not 16.7. Let's use their numbers: they got k_eff = 0.1104 W/m·°C and Q = 16.7 W. I'll present the text's results: they got F_sph=0.005229, Ra=4.776×10⁵, k_eff/k=4.3, k_eff=0.1104, Q=16.7 W. I'll use those.
formula_used: Ra_L = g β ΔT L_c³ Pr / ν²; F_sph = L_c / [(Di Do)^4 (Di^{7/5}+Do^{7/5})^5]; k_eff/k = 0.74 (Pr/(0.861+Pr))^{1/4} (F_sph Ra_L)^{1/4}; Q = π k_eff Di Do ΔT / L_c
trap: 🚨 Using the wrong effective thermal conductivity formula for spheres; forgetting that Q for spherical geometry uses π factor, not 2π.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A horizontal aluminum tube (outer diameter 2 in.) inside a concentric glass tube (4 in. diameter) forms an annular air space. The aluminum tube absorbs solar radiation at 30 Btu/h per foot length. Ambient air is at 70°F. Determine the equilibrium temperature of the aluminum tube when the pump fails (no forced flow).
solution_steps: Step 1: This is a trial‑and‑error problem. Assume glass cover temperature T_g, then compute heat transfer from glass to ambient, and from tube to glass by natural convection in the annulus. Equilibrium when both equal the solar gain.
Step 2: For glass to ambient (horizontal cylinder): Use Churchill-Bernstein correlation for outer cylinder. Estimate T_g=106°F gave Q=30 Btu/h.

Step 3: For annular space: Use correlation for concentric horizontal cylinders. Compute keff, then Q = 2π keff (Ti - To)/ln(Do/Di). Iterate until Q matches 30 Btu/h. Final tube temperature T_tube = 180°F.
formula_used: For outer cylinder: Nu = [0.6 + 0.387 Ra^{1/6}/(1+(0.559/Pr)^{9/16})^{8/27}]²; For annulus: keff/k = 0.386 (Pr/(0.861+Pr))^{1/4} (F_cyl Ra_L)^{1/4}, F_cyl = [ln(Do/Di)]⁴ / [L_c³ (Di^{-3/5}+Do^{-3/5})⁵]; Q = 2π keff (Ti-To)/ln(Do/Di)
trap: 🚨 Neglecting radiation, which is also significant; trial‑and‑error convergence requires careful property evaluation.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Determine the U-factor for the center-of-glass section of a double-pane window with a 6‑mm air space, clear glass (ε=0.84) under winter design conditions. The average air space temperature is 0°C. Use hi=8.29 W/m²·°C, ho=34.0 W/m²·°C.
solution_steps: Step 1: The U-factor for the center section is given by 1/U_center = 1/hi + 1/h_space + 1/ho.
Step 2: The effective emissivity of the air space: ε_eff = 1/(1/ε₁ + 1/ε₂ - 1) = 1/(1/0.84+1/0.84-1) = 0.72.
Step 3: From Table 9‑3 (or interpolation), for 6‑mm air space, ε_eff=0.72, T_ave=0°C, h_space ≈ 7.2 W/m²·°C.
formula_used: 1/U_center = 1/hi + 1/h_space + 1/ho; ε_eff = 1/(1/ε₁ + 1/ε₂ - 1)
trap: 🚌 Using the glass thickness resistance, which is negligible; forgetting to use the correct effective emissivity for the air space.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: A fixed aluminum‑framed window with dimensions 4 ft high × 6 ft wide is to be selected. Determine the rate of heat loss through the window and the inner glass temperature for (a) single glazing, (b) double glazing with ½‑in. air space, and (c) low‑e coated triple glazing with ½‑in. air space. Indoor temperature 72°F, outdoor 15°F, hi = 1.46 Btu/h·ft²·°F.
solution_steps: Step 1: Window area A = 4×6 = 24 ft². U-factors from Table 9‑6 (convert from W/m²·°C to Btu/h·ft²·°F by multiplying by 0.176).

Step 2: (a) Single glazing: U = 6.63×0.176 = 1.166 Btu/h·ft²·°F. Q = U A ΔT = 1.166×24×(72-15) = 1596 Btu/h. Inner glass temperature: T_glass = Ti - Q/(hi A) = 72 - 1596/(1.46×24) = 72 - 45.5 = 26.5°F.
formula_used: Q = U A (Ti - To); T_glass = Ti - Q/(hi A)
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to convert U‑factors from SI to English units; assuming the glass temperature equals room temperature.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Determine the overall U-factor for a double‑door‑type, wood‑framed double‑pane window with metal spacers. Window dimensions: 1.8 m × 2.0 m; each glazing: 1.72 m × 0.94 m. Use given U‑factors for center, edge, and frame.
solution_steps: Step 1: Calculate areas: Awindow = 1.8×2.0 = 3.60 m². Aglazing = 2×(1.72×0.94) = 3.23 m². Aframe = 3.60 - 3.23 = 0.37 m².
Step 2: Edge region width = 6.5 cm (0.065 m). Center dimensions: 1.72-2×0.065 = 1.59 m, 0.94-2×0.065 = 0.81 m. Acenter = 2×(1.59×0.81) = 2.58 m². Aedge = Aglazing - Acenter = 0.65 m².
Step 3: From tables: Ucenter = 3.24 W/m²·°C, Uedge = 3.71 W/m²·°C, Uframe = 2.8 W/m²·°C.
formula_used: U_overall = (Σ U_i A_i) / A_total
trap: 🚨 Assuming the edge region width is the same as the frame width; mis‑calculating the center and edge areas.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Pool Boiling vs Flow Boiling
desc: Pool boiling occurs in a stationary liquid, with fluid motion due to natural convection and bubble buoyancy. Flow boiling involves forced fluid motion by external means (pump, fan), and includes both convection and boiling effects.
trap: 🚨 Confusing the two types when selecting correlations; pool boiling correlations (Rohsenow) are not directly applicable to flow boiling without modification.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Boiling Curve and Regimes
desc: Boiling curve plots heat flux vs excess temperature (Ts – Tsat). Four regimes: natural convection (no bubbles), nucleate boiling (bubbles form and rise), transition boiling (unstable vapor film), and film boiling (stable vapor film). Critical heat flux (CHF) marks the peak before film boiling.
trap: 🚨 Operating beyond CHF in a power‑controlled system leads to sudden temperature rise and burnout; transition boiling is unstable and usually avoided.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Rohsenow Correlation for Nucleate Boiling
desc: Semi‑empirical relation for heat flux during nucleate pool boiling, accounting for surface‑fluid combination via constant C_sf and exponent n. Properties of liquid evaluated at saturation temperature.
formula: q_dot_nucleate = μ_l h_fg [ g(ρ_l - ρ_v)/σ ]^0.5 [ C_pl (T_s - T_sat) / (C_sf h_fg Pr_l^n) ]^3
trap: 🚨 Using wrong C_sf for the specific surface‑fluid combination; also forgetting that n depends on fluid (1.0 for water, 1.7 for many organic fluids).

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Critical Heat Flux (CHF) in Pool Boiling
desc: Maximum heat flux before transition to film boiling. For a large horizontal plate, C_cr ≈ 0.15. The correlation was developed by Kutateladze and Zuber.
formula: q_dot_max = C_cr h_fg [σ g (ρ_l - ρ_v) ρ_v^2]^(1/4)
trap: 🚨 Applying CHF correlation for geometries with small characteristic length without using the correct C_cr from Table 10‑4; also evaluating properties at wrong temperature.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Film Boiling Correlation for Horizontal Cylinder/Sphere
desc: Bromley’s correlation for stable film boiling on horizontal cylinder or sphere. Vapor properties evaluated at film temperature; modified latent heat accounts for vapor superheating.
formula: q_dot_film = C_film [ g k_v^3 ρ_v (ρ_l - ρ_v) (h_fg + 0.4 C_pv (T_s - T_sat)) / (μ_v D (T_s - T_sat)) ]^(1/4)
trap: 🚨 Using liquid properties instead of vapor properties; also neglecting radiation at high surface temperatures, which can be significant.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Nusselt Film Condensation on Vertical Plate
desc: Classical analysis for laminar film condensation on a vertical plate assuming linear temperature profile, negligible vapor drag, and laminar flow. Uses modified latent heat h_fg* = h_fg + 0.68 C_pl (T_sat - T_s).
formula: h_vert = 0.943 [ g ρ_l (ρ_l - ρ_v) k_l^3 h_fg* / (μ_l L (T_sat - T_s)) ]^(1/4)
trap: 🚨 Using the original h_fg without correction for subcooling; also applying for Reynolds numbers above 30 without checking flow regime.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Condensate Reynolds Number
desc: Defined as Re = 4 m_dot / (p μ_l), where m_dot is condensate mass flow rate at the bottom and p is wetted perimeter. For vertical plates, Re = 4 h L (T_sat - T_s) / (μ_l h_fg*). Laminar wave‑free: Re < 30; wavy laminar: 30–1800; turbulent: >1800.
formula: Re = 4 Γ / μ_l, with Γ = mass flow rate per unit width
trap: 🚌 Using diameter instead of hydraulic diameter for non‑vertical geometries; also misidentifying regime leads to wrong correlation.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Horizontal Tube Film Condensation
desc: Nusselt analysis for laminar film condensation on a horizontal tube. Heat transfer coefficient is inversely proportional to the fourth root of diameter.
formula: h_horiz = 0.729 [ g ρ_l (ρ_l - ρ_v) k_l^3 h_fg* / (μ_l D (T_sat - T_s)) ]^(1/4)
trap: 🚨 Forgetting to divide by N^(1/4) for vertical tiers of tubes; also using the same constant (0.729) for spheres (should be 0.815).

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Effect of Noncondensable Gases on Condensation
desc: Even small amounts of noncondensable gas (e.g., air) in the vapor drastically reduce condensation heat transfer by forming a stagnant layer near the surface, impeding vapor diffusion.
trap: 🚨 Ignoring the presence of noncondensable gases in vacuum condensers; periodic venting is required to maintain performance.

subject: Heat Transfer
name: Dropwise Condensation
desc: Condensation in discrete droplets instead of a continuous film, yielding heat transfer coefficients 10 times higher than film condensation. Difficult to sustain; requires promoters (e.g., oleic acid) or coatings.
formula: h_dropwise = 51,104 + 2044 T_sat (for steam on copper, T_sat < 100°C)
trap: 🚨 Assuming dropwise condensation can be maintained indefinitely without promoters or special surface treatment; it usually reverts to film condensation.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Water is to be boiled at atmospheric pressure in a mechanically polished stainless steel pan placed on top of a heating unit. The inner surface of the bottom of the pan is maintained at 108°C. If the diameter of the bottom of the pan is 30 cm, determine (a) the rate of heat transfer to the water and (b) the rate of evaporation of water.
solution_steps: Step 1: Determine excess temperature ΔT = T_s - T_sat = 108°C - 100°C = 8°C. This is within nucleate boiling regime.
Step 2: Gather water properties at T_sat = 100°C: ρ_l = 957.9 kg/m³, ρ_v = 0.6 kg/m³, h_fg = 2257×10³ J/kg, μ_l = 0.282×10⁻³ kg/m·s, Pr_l = 1.75, C_pl = 4217 J/kg·°C, σ = 0.0589 N/m.
Step 3: For water‑stainless steel (mechanically polished), C_sf = 0.0130, n = 1.0.
Step 4: Apply Rohsenow correlation: q_dot = μ_l h_fg [g(ρ_l-ρ_v)/σ]^0.5 [C_plΔT/(C_sf h_fg Pr_l^n)]³.
Step 7: Area A = πD²/4 = π×0.3²/4 = 0.07069 m². Heat transfer Q_dot = q_dot × A = 7.20×10⁴ × 0.07069 ≈ 5093 W.
Step 8: Evaporation rate m_dot = Q_dot / h_fg = 5093 / (2257×10³) ≈ 0.00226 kg/s.
formula_used: q_dot_nucleate = μ_l h_fg [g(ρ_l-ρ_v)/σ]^0.5 [C_plΔT/(C_sf h_fg Pr_l^n)]³
final_answer: Heat transfer rate = 5.09 kW; Evaporation rate = 2.26 g/s
trap: 🚨 Using the wrong C_sf for the surface‑fluid combination; also forgetting to convert diameter to meters.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Water in a tank is to be boiled at sea level by a 1‑cm‑diameter nickel plated steel heating element. Determine the maximum heat flux that can be attained in the nucleate boiling regime and the surface temperature of the heater surface in that case.
solution_steps: Step 1: For a short horizontal cylinder, characteristic length L = radius = 0.005 m.
Step 3: Apply critical heat flux formula: q_dot_max = C_cr h_fg [σ g (ρ_l-ρ_v) ρ_v²]^(1/4). Using properties from Example 10‑1: h_fg = 2257×10³ J/kg, ρ_v = 0.6 kg/m³, etc.

Step 5: To find T_s, use Rohsenow equation solved for ΔT: q_dot_max = μ_l h_fg [g(ρ_l-ρ_v)/σ]^0.5 [C_plΔT/(C_sf h_fg Pr_l^n)]³. Substitute known values and solve for ΔT. For nickel plated steel, C_sf = 0.0060, n = 1.0.
Step 6: Rearranged: ΔT = C_sf h_fg Pr_l [ q_dot_max / (μ_l h_fg [g(ρ_l-ρ_v)/σ]^0.5 ) ]^(1/3) / C_pl.
formula_used: q_dot_max = C_cr h_fg [σ g (ρ_l-ρ_v) ρ_v²]^(1/4); Rohsenow equation for ΔT
final_answer: Maximum heat flux = 1.02×10⁶ W/m²; Surface temperature = 119°C
trap: 🚌 Using C_cr for large horizontal plate (0.149) instead of the correct value for a small cylinder (0.12) leads to overestimation.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Water is boiled at atmospheric pressure by a horizontal polished copper heating element of diameter D = 5 mm and emissivity ε = 0.05 immersed in water. If the surface temperature of the heating wire is 350°C, determine the rate of heat transfer from the wire to the water per unit length of the wire.
solution_steps: Step 1: Excess temperature ΔT = 350°C – 100°C = 250°C, so film boiling occurs.
Step 2: Vapor properties at film temperature T_f = (350+100)/2 = 225°C ≈ 500 K. From tables: ρ_v = 0.441 kg/m³, k_v = 0.0357 W/m·K, μ_v = 1.73×10⁻⁵ kg/m·s, C_pv = 1977 J/kg·K.
Step 3: Liquid properties at T_sat = 100°C: ρ_l = 957.9 kg/m³, h_fg = 2257×10³ J/kg.
Step 5: For a horizontal cylinder, C_film = 0.62. Compute q_dot_film = 0.62 [ g k_v³ ρ_v (ρ_l-ρ_v) h_fg* / (μ_v D ΔT) ]^(1/4) × ΔT.
Step 8: Then q_dot_film = 0.62 × 382.8 × 250 ≈ 59,300 W/m².
Step 10: Total heat flux q_dot_total = q_dot_film + (3/4) q_dot_rad ≈ 59,300 + 122 ≈ 59,422 W/m².
Step 11: Area per unit length = πD = π×0.005 = 0.01571 m²/m. Q_dot per unit length = 59,422 × 0.01571 ≈ 933 W/m.
formula_used: q_dot_film = C_film [g k_v³ ρ_v (ρ_l-ρ_v) h_fg* / (μ_v D ΔT)]^(1/4) ΔT; q_dot_rad = εσ(T_s⁴–T_sat⁴)
final_answer: Heat transfer rate = 933 W per meter of wire
trap: 🚨 Using liquid properties instead of vapor properties in film boiling correlation; also forgetting to account for radiation at high temperatures (here it was small due to low ε).

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure condenses on a 2‑m‑high and 3‑m‑wide vertical plate that is maintained at 80°C by circulating cooling water. Determine (a) the rate of heat transfer by condensation to the plate and (b) the rate at which the condensate drips off the plate at the bottom.
solution_steps: Step 1: T_sat = 100°C, T_s = 80°C. Film temperature T_f = (100+80)/2 = 90°C.
Step 2: Liquid properties at 90°C: ρ_l = 965.3 kg/m³, μ_l = 0.315×10⁻³ kg/m·s, k_l = 0.675 W/m·K, C_pl = 4206 J/kg·K, ν_l = μ_l/ρ_l = 0.326×10⁻⁶ m²/s.
Step 6: Heat transfer area A_s = width × height = 3×2 = 6 m². Q_dot = h A_s (T_sat – T_s) = 628.8×6×20 = 75,456 W ≈ 75.5 kW.
Step 7: Condensate mass flow rate m_dot = Q_dot / h_fg* = 75,456 / (2.314×10⁶) ≈ 0.0326 kg/s.
final_answer: Heat transfer rate = 75.5 kW; Condensate rate = 0.0326 kg/s
trap: 🚨 Using the laminar (wave‑free) correlation for Re ≈ 936 would give a lower h; the wavy‑laminar correlation gives about 20% higher.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: Repeat Example 10‑4 for the case where the plate is tilted 30° from the vertical.
solution_steps: Step 1: For inclined plates, use the vertical plate correlation with g replaced by g cosθ. Since we already have h_vert = 628.8 W/m²·K from Example 10‑4 (vertical case), use h_inclined = h_vert (cosθ)^(1/4).
Step 3: h_inclined = 628.8 × 0.965 ≈ 607 W/m²·K.
Step 4: Heat transfer Q_dot = h_inclined A_s (T_sat – T_s) = 607×6×20 = 72,840 W.
Step 5: Condensate rate m_dot = Q_dot / h_fg* = 72,840 / (2.314×10⁶) ≈ 0.0315 kg/s.
formula_used: h_inclined = h_vert (cosθ)^(1/4)
final_answer: Heat transfer rate = 72.8 kW; Condensate rate = 0.0315 kg/s
trap: 🚨 Using (cosθ) instead of (cosθ)^(1/4) would underestimate h too much.

subject: Heat Transfer
problem: The condenser of a steam power plant operates at a pressure of 7.38 kPa (T_sat = 40°C). Steam condenses on the outer surfaces of horizontal pipes of outer diameter 3 cm, maintained at 30°C. Determine (a) the rate of heat transfer to the cooling water per unit length of a pipe and (b) the rate of condensation of steam per unit length.
solution_steps: Step 1: Film temperature T_f = (40+30)/2 = 35°C. Liquid properties at 35°C: ρ_l = 994 kg/m³, μ_l = 0.720×10⁻³ kg/m·s, k_l = 0.623 W/m·K, C_pl = 4178 J/kg·K.
Step 2: h_fg at T_sat = 40°C = 2407×10³ J/kg (from steam tables). Modified h_fg* = h_fg + 0.68 C_pl (T_sat – T_s) = 2407×10³ + 0.68×4178×10 = 2407×10³ + 28,410 = 2.435×10⁶ J/kg.
Step 3: Neglect ρ_v relative to ρ_l. Use Nusselt horizontal tube correlation: h_horiz = 0.729 [ g ρ_l² k_l³ h_fg* / (μ_l D (T_sat – T_s)) ]^(1/4).
Step 7: Area per unit length A_s/L = πD = π×0.03 = 0.09425 m²/m. Q_dot/L = h A_s (T_sat – T_s) = 9260×0.09425×10 = 8725 W/m.
Step 8: Condensate rate per unit length m_dot/L = (Q_dot/L) / h_fg* = 8725 / (2.435×10⁶) = 0.00358 kg/s·m.
formula_used: h_horiz = 0.729 [g ρ_l² k_l³ h_fg* / (μ_l D (T_sat – T_s))]^(1/4)
